Patients who experienced a longer progression-free survival (PFS) exhibited a higher proportion of arrested cells within each cell phenotype.

Variations in the proportion of proliferation states were observed among cancer cells, stromal cells, and CD8+ T cells when comparing samples with short and long PFS.

Spatial distances and co-occurrence patterns exhibited distinct profiles in pre- and post-neoadjuvant chemotherapy (NACT) samples. Furthermore, these patterns also varied between post-NACT samples with long and short PFS.

INTRODUCTION
MATERIAL AND METHODS
CONTACT INFO
ACKNOWLEDGEMENTS
REFRENCES
In high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma (HGSC), tumors with HRD derive the most significant benefit from poly (ADP-ribose) polymerase inhibitor (PARPi) therapy. However, accurate testing strategies still remain unclear. Additionally, some HRD patients progress during first-line PARPi maintenance therapy. Molecular alterations behind PARPi resistance remain inconclusive.
Our study aims to answer the questions: 
Are core needle biopsies suitable for routine HRD testing?How does neo-adjuvant chemotherapy affect the success of HRD testing? 
When to use functional HRD testing in the clinical setting?

A cohort of 332 Oncosys-Ova (NCT06117384) patients were subjected to different HRD testing modalities at different stages of treatment and analyzed for clinical outcomes.
CONCLUSIONS
Number of NACT cycles affect the success rate of genomic HRD testing from surgical samples
2. Pre-NACT-biopsies are sufficient for HRD testing
3. Real-world data confirms prologned PFS on HRD patients
4. Functional HRD testing predicts clinical outcomes and complements genomic testing
5. RNAseq and WES analyses for patients who progressed during PARPi or completed two years of treatment without progression
Aim: to establish optimal homologous recombination deficiency (HRD) testing strategies and to characterize genomic features driving progression during first-line PARPi treatment.
53% (n = 10) of the biopsy-only tested patients did not have sufficient amount of vital tumor for genomic HRD testing in the IDS sample.
Biopsy samples were successful in 87% (n = 13) of cases, whereas IDS samples in 77% (n=10). All successful IDS samples had concordant biopsy result.
In our prospective patient cohort patients were genomically tested using ovaHRDscar

High ovaHRDscar score predicts more favorable prognosis among HRD patients compared to patient with low score.

The low positive threshold is 68, corresponding to the median score of HRD-positive (≥54) tumors tested with ovaHRDscar (n=123).

We performed fHR testing of 19 samples.
Preliminary analyses show favorable survival status for fHRD patients; however, further analyses are on-going.

9 of the patients were treated with PARPi in the 1st line and 10 in the 2nd line, and we performed separate survival analyses for the different treatment lines.
We performed WES and RNA-sequencing for 18 samples from 1st line PARPi-treated patients, of which 11 had progressed during the treatment whereas 7 had completed at least two years of treatment.

Copy number variation frequencies are shown per segment, with the upper segment representing patients with progression during PARPi therapy and the lower panel representing patients who completed the maintenance treatment.

Preliminary analysis does not detect differences in genomic alterations between patient groups (progression vs no progression).

B7-H4
TME landscape and spatial immune niches 
were proﬁled in 80 chemo-naïve patients with 
HGSC enrolled in the prospective Oncosys-
OVA clinical trial (NCT06117384). 
RCNs stratify clinical subgroups (HRD vs. HRP),
with HRP tumours enriched in B-cell, Stromal-CAF, 
and stroma-resident immune niches, consistent 
with SPACEstat analysis.
tCycIF spatial analysis conﬁrms 
heterogeneous expression of ADC 
targets (B7-H4, TROP2, FOLR1) in 
HGSC, with distinct and 
overlapping TME localisation, 
supporting ongoing eﬀorts to 
map target distribution, assess 
ADC-driven TME modulation, and 
inform patient stratiﬁcation, 
eﬃcacy, and safety.
Recurrent driver alterations co-localise with characteristic copy number 
alteration (CNA) signature events (G-score peaks) and are broadly 
dispersed across the genome, consistent with the HGSC CNV landscape; 
this suggests TME structures are associated with broader CNA signature 
clusters rather than single focal drivers, enabling patient stratiﬁcation 
detectable by tCycIF.
In contrast, somatic SNVs and germline variants are relatively sparse on 
the oncoplot, indicating a limited contribution compared with the 
dominant CNA-driven signatures that potentially underlie the observed 
TME phenotypes.
In HGSC, prognosis is strongly inﬂuenced 
by the spatial organisation of the tumour 
microenvironment (TME); applying the

combining tissue-based cyclic

## Slide 1
Acknowledgements
Introduction
Cervical intraepithelial neoplasia grade 2 (CIN2) is a precancerous lesion caused by persistent high-risk human papillomavirus (HPV) infection, with 50–60% of CIN2 lesions regressing spontaneously. The mechanisms behind regression remain poorly understood, limiting the prediction of which lesions will regress. This study integrates histopathology, spatial proteomics, and transcriptomic profiling to uncover characteristics that drive CIN2 regression.
CONTACT
E. Anttila 1, N. Guruprasad 1,4 , O. Lehtonen1, K. Zhang1,3, Z. Liang1 , N. Nordlund1, S. Shah1, L. Bergqvist2, K. Aro2, I. Niemic1, S. Hautaniemi1, I. Kalliala2,5, A. Färkkilä*1,2, A. Virtanen*1,3 *correspondence
1Research Program in Systems Oncology, University of Helsinki, Faculty of Medicine, Finland, 2Department of Obstetrics and Gynecology, Helsinki University Hospital, Helsinki, Finland, 3Department of Pathology, University of Helsinki and HUS Diagnostic Center, Helsinki University Hospital, Finland, 4Institute for Molecular Medicine Finland, Helsinki Institute of Life Sciences, University of Helsinki, Finland, 5Department of Metabolism, Digestion and Reproduction, Faculty of Medicine, Institute of Reproductive and Developmental Biology, Imperial College, London, UK.
Methods
Results
Conclusions
Our machine‑learning pipeline integrating quantitative histological, clinical, and HPV data reveals that lesion size, HPV genotype, immune features, and nuclear morphology are the strongest predictors of CIN2 regression.

t‑CyCIF profiling with a 22‑antibody panel captured 3.8 million cells, revealing substantial heterogeneity in cellular composition.

GeoMx spatial transcriptomics generated 243 ROIs (400–500 cells each) across lesion, stroma, and lesion–stroma interfaces.

8: iCAN- Digital Precision Cancer Medicine, University of Helsinki, Helsinki, Finland
*: These authors contributed equally to this work and share last authorship.
Introduction
High-Grade Serous Carcinoma (HGSC) features high spatial heterogeneity, genomic instability, and poor survival. The relationship between transcriptional states and protein execution, especially in chemotherapy-selected cells, is unclear. We aim to identify and characterize "subsister cells": cancer cells that survive first-line chemotherapy and cause relapse. By integrating proteomic and transcriptomic data, we will define the functional states and spatial organization of these subsister cells to uncover new biology states and discover new drug targets.
Methods

first-line chemotherapy and cause relapse. By integrating proteomic and transcriptomic data, we will define the functional states and spatial organization of these subsister cells to uncover new biology states and discover new drug targets.
Methods
We performed a comprehensive multi-omic spatial study on fresh tumor tissue and FFPE blocks from 16 HGSC patients undergoing primary or interval debulking surgery from the Oncosys-ova trial. Our unique approach integrates paired single-cell RNA sequencing with single-cell spatial transcriptomics (Xenium) and spatial proteomics (t-CyCIF) performed on the same tissue sections.
Results
Initial single-cell RNAseq analysis of our paired dataset revealed multiple distinct functional tumor phenotypes (e.g., stress-response, pro-inflammatory/IFN-response, extracellular matrix remodeling), whose spatial localization was subsequently validated by spatial transcriptomics. Before integration, we studied the correlation patterns between transcription and protein expression across tumor regions, which enabled us to identify preliminary cross-modality relationships. This demonstrated strong concordance between transcript density and corresponding protein expression across tumor regions. To achieve our aim of further resolving cross-modality relationships and dissecting post-transcriptional regulation from gene expression, we are deploying CAVACHON, a hierarchical variational autoencoder. This novel framework is designed to integrate single-cell multi-omics. By conditioning protein abundance on gene expression, which will enable the dissection of post-transcriptional regulation from gene expression programs and spatially anchor transcriptional states to tumor niches.
Conclusions

single-cell multi-omics. By conditioning protein abundance on gene expression, which will enable the dissection of post-transcriptional regulation from gene expression programs and spatially anchor transcriptional states to tumor niches.
Conclusions
This integrative approach will allow us to spatially anchor transcriptional programs to tumor niches, identifying clinically relevant phenotypes and spatial niches of subsister cells. Overall, our work enables the identification of genes and pathways differentially activated across spatial tumor regions; the resolution of cross-modality interactions and spatial dependencies beyond single-modality analyses; and the isolation of a chemotherapy-response prognostic signature for validation in external cohorts.

Tertiary lymphoid structures (TLS) are immune formations in
chronically inflamed tissues. TLSs and tumor-infiltrating B-cells are
linked to improved therapy outcomes in several cancers. However,
their origin, prevalence, spatial distribution, functional stages,
molecular underpinnings, and clinical relevance remain unknown in
ovarian cancer subtypes.

High-grade serous ovarian cancer (HGSC) tumors are
complex ecosystems where tumor–stromal–immune
interactions shape therapeutic responses. Cancer-
associated fibroblasts (CAFs) are major regulators in this
dynamic crosstalk, yet their spatially distinct functional
roles remain poorly understood. Using single-cell and
spatial transcriptomics alongside patient-derived ex
vivo models, we are mapping how CAF programs are
organized at the HGSC tumor–stroma interface (TSI),
and how this organization influences therapy sensitivity.

Input signal

Prior knowledge - association with a disease
The signal could be sparse!

## Slide 10
Network propagation
Input signal
Question: How could we leverage the network topology to identify other molecules that may be affected by or associated with the studied context?

## Slide 11
Network propagation
Network propagation is a computational approach to amplify the signal of distinct nodes (seeds) on the network, in order to identify other nodes, which are significantly accessible to that signal

## Slide 19
Random walk with restart
Hypothesis:

Three seed nodes: 14, 17 & 29

A walker traverses the network:
Option A (with probability d): To move randomly to one of the adjacent nodes
Option B (with probability 1-d): To be teletransported to a randomly selected seed node and restart the walk
Walker

## Slide 20
Random walk with restart
Hypothesis:

Three seed nodes: 14, 17 & 29

A walker traverses the network:
Option A (with probability d): To move randomly to one of the adjacent nodes
Option B (with probability 1-d): To be teletransported to a randomly selected seed node and restart the walk

d is called “damping factor” and controls to what extent the walker goes away from the seed nodes
Walker

## Slide 21
Random walk with restart
Hypothesis:

Three seed nodes: 14, 17 & 29

A walker traverses the network:
Option A (with probability d): To move randomly to one of the adjacent nodes
Option B (with probability 1-d): To be teletransported to a randomly selected seed node and restart the walk

We count how many times the walker passes through each node, in order to find the most visited ones
Walker

Betweenness centrality
Definition: The betweenness centrality of node i is equal to the proportion of the shortest paths that connect any pair of nodes and pass through i

Indicates nodes that function as “bridges” in the network, connecting different parts of it

## Slide 28
PageRank centrality
Definition: The PageRank (PR) of node i is equal to the weighted average PageRank of its neighbors, regulated by a factor d:

page_rank(graph, vids=V(graph), damping=0.85, directed=False, weights=None,...)🔗

Regulation by d
V are the network nodes
Ei are the neighbors of node i

eriments were performed to validate the impact of these markers (prevent or promote ED)

## Slide 66
Case study: Endothelial Dysfunction in CV Disorders
Network propagation was used to detect the underlying subnetworks of anti-ED and pro-ED markers:
26 pro-ED genes -> pro-ED network with 176 nodes
31 anti-ED genes -> anti-ED network with 167 nodes

## Slide 67
Case study: Endothelial Dysfunction in CV Disorders
Network propagation was used to detect the underlying subnetworks of anti-ED and pro-ED markers:
26 pro-ED genes -> pro-ED network with 176 nodes
31 anti-ED genes -> anti-ED network with 167 nodes
The enrichment analysis of pro-ED network signature verified the strong correlation between endothelial dysfunction and inflammation

## Slide 68
Conclusion
Network propagation can be used to amplify the sparse signal/information for specific nodes on the network
It is based on the concepts of personalized PageRank and RWR
The final predictions depend on the selection and weighting of seeds, damping factor and network topology
Statistical significance can be inferred using randomized networks
It has been used in various contexts/scenarios in biomedical studies
phuEGO is a network-propagation tool, used to analyze sparse omics data in order to detect context-specific active signalling pathways/modules

text:
## Page 1
Multi-Omics Factor Analysis (MOFA) 
A general framework for the unsupervised integration of 
multi-omic data sets 
Materials adapted from 
Ricard Argelaguet 
rargelaguet@altoslabs.com 
Senior Scientist, Altos Labs UK
Ricardo O. Ramirez Flores 
flores@ebi.ac.uk 
Staff Scientist, EMBL-EBI

## Page 2
What are the problems of CCA for multi-omics data integration?
In CCA the canonical components are defined as linear combinations of 
features that maximise the cross-correlation between the two data sets. 
This implies that: 
•It only works for the integration of 2 data sets 
•It only finds sources of covariation between the two data sets. CCA is 
not able to find the sources of variation that are present within 
individual data sets

## Page 3
Multi-Omics Factor Analysis (MOFA)
Argelaguet & Velten et al. Mol Sys Bio. 2018 
•The structure of the 
data is specified in the 
prior distributions of the 
Bayesian model 
•The critical part of the 
model is the use 
sparsity priors, which 
enable automatic 
relevance determination 
of the factors

@KorcsmarosLab
Multiple ways to integrate multi-omics data: 
case studies from studying human diseases
4 March 2026
TAMAS KORCSMAROS
Associate Professor, Division of Digestive Diseases
Head, Imperial Organoid Facility
Director of Centre for Intestinal Systems
Introduction to Multiomics Data Integration 
and Visualisation, EMBL-EBI

## Page 2
@KorcsmarosLab
https://github.com/KorcsmarosGroup/
http://KorcsmarosLab.org


## Page 4
The Korcsmaros group
Goal of the group: To understand biological systems (cell-cell 
and cell-microbe interactomes) related to gut homeostasis 
and to facilitate precision medicine and personalised 
microbial therapies for inflammatory bowel disease (IBD).


## Page 5
Overview
• From pathways to networks
➢Protein-protein interactions (PPIs) 
and regulation
➢Network Medicine
+ Genomics (mutation)
+ Transcriptomics (expression)
+ Bulk
+ Single-cell
 
+ Metabolic reactions 

## Page 6
• “Network medicine” (Barabasi, 2007)
• “Systems medicine is finally coming of age” (Lemberger, 2007) 
• Network as the target (Pawson and Linding, 2008)
• “Think globally, act locally” (Barabasi, Loscalzo, 2011)
• Nowadays considered as a resource for
– biomarker discovery
– drug target prediction
– drug side-effect analysis
– drug repurposing
– suggesting 
new therapies
– patient-stratification
Network medicine – a promising history
Korcsmaros et al, Integrative Biology, 2017

and data integration?

Networks can be organised into 
layers depending on the kind of 
interactions they contain.
These multi-layered networks

picture about how the system 
works.
Multi-layered networks provide 
a scaffold to integrate and 
interpret multi-omics data.

## Page 23
Organoids are in vitro primary 
cultures capable of self-renewal 
and self-organisation, and 
exhibiting similar three-
dimensional structure and 
functionality as the tissue of 
origin. 
Organoids: What they are and why they matter
Traditional biomedical models and their limitations
• Immortalised cell lines: cancer related, too homogenic, too simple
• Animal models: too complex, often not translatable to human, ethical considerations
• Human tissue: restricted access and availability, only short-term viable
Challenges with working with organoids
• Infrastructure for organoid culture
• Access to organoid lines
• Expensive running costs
• Expertise and training options

## Page 24
Digestion 
problems
Pain
Tiredness
Inflammatory Bowel Disease (IBD)
• Pathogenesis: mostly unknown
• Dysregulation of immune 
and inflammation responses
• Intestinal microbiome (diet)
• Genetic (several autophagy genes)
• Two major types
• Crohn’s disease (CD)
• Ulcerative colitis (UC)
• >9 million people worldwide 
• 5.5 million patients in the US, 500,000 in the UK 
• $8.5 billion annual cost in 2018
• Treatments: ineffective due to frequent relapse
• Pathogenic role for cell types or microbes is unclear
• Too many data on mouse models 
Change in cell-cell and cell-microbe interactions

• IBD-associated SNPs may contribute to disease pathogenesis by rewiring the 
incoming signals which regulate gene expression.
• Diverse non-coding SNP combinations in IBD patients led to functional switches from 
multiple healthy incoming signals to fewer disease-associated signals.
• Personalised investigation of signalling processes upstream of genetic 
polymorphisms can provide a more comprehensive understanding of IBD 
pathogenesis including gene-environment interactions.
What is the situation downstream of the SNPs?
Bohar et al, Journal of Crohn’s & Colitis, 2025

## Page 57
Conclusions of the first neighbour analysis
• First neighbours of cancer-related proteins are at least as 
important as cancer-related proteins themselves. 
• Complementary strategy in cancer to rewire signalling 
networks:
– Mutations directly affecting the more central proteins
– Differential expression affecting the first neighbours of central 
proteins

## Page 34
Representing networks

## Page 35
One of the most commonly used 
representation due to its ease of use, and the 
ability to use it directly for measurements is 
the adjacency matrix. 
For an undirected network, if the position 
A12 equals to one, that means there exists 
an interaction between nodes 1 and 2.
For directed networks the direction of the 
interaction carries information on its own: 1 
→ 3 is not the same as 1 ← 3, and their 
adjacency matrices behave accordingly. 
Representing networks
Figure: network science book

used format is the edge 
list. 
The edge list is more 
human readable, and is 
easy to write up on the 
ﬂy. 
The example uses a .csv 
format, but diﬀerent 
software of course can 
accept diﬀerent 
delimiters.

## Page 42
Most real-world networks have a degree 
distribution like the following
degre
e
frequency
Most nodes have small degrees: only one or two interactions
However, a few nodes have a very large number of interactions, 
these are the hubs
They are called hubs, because when you visualize them in a 
network, they look like the hubs of a wagon wheel
On the internet hubs are webpages like Google or Amazon
Can we think of a few biological examples from your studies?

Dangers and pitfalls

## Page 68
• Biological networks almost always have to deal with a huge 
study bias
• some proteins / interactions will be more studied – can we 
think of a few examples?
• because of this, they will show up more frequently in our 
network resources
Study bias

## Page 69
• Trivial predictions can dominate your results, especially if you

chunk_id: Conference abstracts and materials/Introduction to multi-omics data integration and visualisation 2026_EMBL/Contents/08_Introduction to networks and pathways.pdf::chunk_0008
source_file: Conference abstracts and materials/Introduction to multi-omics data integration and visualisation 2026_EMBL/Contents/08_Introduction to networks and pathways.pdf
text:
proteins / interactions will be more studied – can we 
think of a few examples?
• because of this, they will show up more frequently in our 
network resources
Study bias

## Page 69
• Trivial predictions can dominate your results, especially if you 
are familiar with the biological domain (this is also a result of 
study bias)
• You will probably make a long list of predictions (that will be 
correct) that you have to go through to ﬁnd novel information
Trivial predictions

## Page 70
• Biological networks can have simultaneously many false 
negative / false positive hits
• Depending on your experimental method, studied organism 
etc. the coverage of your interaction networks can vary quite 
a lot
• Therefore using path based metrics such as shortest paths 
or betweenness centrality can be misleading, as even the 
addition / loss of one edge can shift these results a lot
Path fragility

## Page 77
Visualising networks - the good news
● Networks are great at describing relationships between items
● They are well suited for tasks involving topology:
○ The direct and indirect paths between nodes, and what they 
mean
○ Small networks are very intuitive, easily understood

## Page 78
Visualising networks - the bad news
● The scalability of layouts is not very good - increasing the amount of 
nodes and edges quickly turns into a hairball 
● Most layout algorithms are nondeterministic: you get a slightly 
diﬀerent result every time you run them


## Page 79
Questions to ask
Establishing what do we want to investigate: 
● The whole network? 
● A node subset? 
● Key edges? 
● Communities? 
● What is the story we want to tell with the visualization?

Carcinoma
Nika Mikhailava¹, María Hincapié-Otero¹, Joonas Jukonen¹, Iga Niemiec¹, Zhihan Liang¹, Aleksandra Shabanova¹, Matilda Salko¹, Ulla-Maija Haltia¹², Anni Virtanen¹³, Anniina Färkkilä¹²³⁴
¹ Research Program in Systems Oncology, University of Helsinki, Helsinki, Finland ² Department of Obstetrics and Gynecology, Department of Oncology, Clinical Trials Unit, Comprehensive Cancer Center, Helsinki University Hospital, Helsinki, Finland ³ Department of Pathology, 
University of Helsinki and HUS Diagnostic Center, Helsinki University Hospital, Helsinki, Finland ⁴ Institute for Molecular Medicine Finland, Helsinki Institute for Life Sciences, University of Helsinki, Helsinki, Finland
Created with BioRender Poster Builder
Background
● High- grade serous 
carcinoma (HGSC) is the 
deadliest ovarian cancer 
subtype.
● Resistance to treatment 
develops in 70-80% of 
cases.
● Cancer- associated 
fibroblasts (CAFs) drive 
treatment resistance.
● Targeting CAF pathways 
offers new treatment 
opportunities. 
Data and Methods
● Stroma and Tumor- Stroma Interface ( TSI) Regions of 
Interest (ROIs) selected for analysis.
● Fibroblast- specific gene expression deconvoluted 
using BayesPrism algorithm.
AOIs Selection and Annotation 
 
611 Stroma Areas of 
Illumination (AOIs) 
were profiled (309 TSI, 
302 distant stroma)
 ROI from TSI
Clustering of CAFs by Variable Genes
Distinct transcriptional profiles of CAFs in 
TSI and Stroma.
Cell Abundance Across TSI-Stroma and Distant Stroma AOIs
● CAFs proportions 
similar between TSI 
and stroma -> 
expression 
differences not due 
to abundance.
● Immune cell 
populations differ 
significantly 
-> altered immune

in 
TSI and Stroma.
Cell Abundance Across TSI-Stroma and Distant Stroma AOIs
● CAFs proportions 
similar between TSI 
and stroma -> 
expression 
differences not due 
to abundance.
● Immune cell 
populations differ 
significantly 
-> altered immune 
signaling.
 Gene Expression Differences in TSI and Distant Stroma
● TSI : ↑ Interferon response (ISG15, IFI6), invasion 
signaling (PKN3), ECM remodeling (COLA1, FN1).
● Stroma : ↑ Chemokines (CCL19, CCL13, CXCL5), ECM 
remodeling (MMP8).
 
GSEA analysis between TSI and Stroma CAFs
Conclusions and Perspectives
● Highlighted the value of spatial profiling in distinguishing TSI and distant 
stroma fibroblasts .
● Uncovered genes and pathways that differentiate fibroblast functions in 
TSI and stroma .
● Future work : Integrate findings with scRNAseq and validate in icPDC co-
culture models.

Summary of multi-omics data: publicly available scRNA-seq 
(n = 7) and visium spatial transcriptomics data (n = 2)2. High-plex 
cyclic immunofluorescence imaging (CyCIF) of 345 tissue 
microarray (TMA) cores from primary TNBC samples 
(n=186 patients), these cores from different histological regions,
including 192 tumor center, 66 invasive border, 61 inflamed
regions, and 26 lymph node metastasis. 
Our results demonstrate that the spatial distribution of oxidative stress response genes and proteins in tumors is diverse and 
can have important clinical implications. The identified cell type-specific antioxidant expression profiles can serve as a future 
resource when designing molecules to target the high antioxidant state.

## Slide 10
Examples of artifacts – Linear External Artefacts EA
External artifacts (dust particles, hair shafts, fibers) can be found within the tissue, outside the tissue, or spanning across both the internal organizational space and the external background.

## Slide 11
Examples of artifacts – Nonlinear EA

## Slide 12
Examples of artifacts – NOT an EA
There are two long strips that resemble EA, but upon closer inspection, they have distinct tissue-like structures inside and lack transparency

## Slide 13
Examples of artifacts – Out Of Focus OOF
OOF is when a part of tissue is blurred. There are 2 most common types of OOF. In the first one the entire tile is out of focus and the blurred region is rectangular. In the second one the tissue is not well attached to the slide and some of it detaches generating less regular out of focus part

## Slide 14
Examples of artifacts – Tissue Fold TF
Tissue folds are essentially a special type of out-of-focus feature. Imagine the tissue as folded origami in your mind. When annotating, only select the parts where two layers of tissue are folded.

## Slide 14
Examples of artifacts – Tissue Fold TF
Tissue folds are essentially a special type of out-of-focus feature. Imagine the tissue as folded origami in your mind. When annotating, only select the parts where two layers of tissue are folded. Do not choose the blank areas (the black background), as the original tissue has been folded away to another location.

## Slide 15
Examples of artifacts – Air Bubble AB
AB is a transparent circle

## Slide 16
Examples of artifacts – Antiboody Aggregate AA
Antibody Aggreagates look like fireworks, with the center being very bright and small sparks seemingly surrounding it. It’s important to note that AA will never appear alone. If it exists in a certain area, there must be at least a few or even dozens of AA points in that region

Hold shift to enlarge the annotated region, shift+alt to diminish/earse it
Annotations
There are 2 main tools for making the annotations. The first one, Brush, is useful to annotate bigger regions. The second, Wand, is trying to detect the edges of the objects, which is helpful with more detailed annotations. 
Be as precise and accurate as possible, which almost pixel-like precision

## Slide 24
Exporting and importing annotations to other images
This is a useful way to export annotation for the artefact that is present in multiple channels, such as external artifacts. So, if you annotated a hair in DAPI channel you can export it to all other channel.

## Slide 25
Exporting and importing annotations to other images
Go to 'Project' and select the picture to which you want to export the annotatons. Click again on 'Edit' and select 'Paste'. Your annotations should appear on the image now

## Slide 26

Exporting finished annotations

## Slide 27
Exporting finished annotations
Copy the script 'Export annotations.groovy' to your device. Then go to 'Automate' --> 'Script editor'. Load the script (on Mac: drag and drop the scrip) and change the names within the scripts to the class name that you want to export (see the screenshot, in that case I changed them to "EA")

## Slide 28
Exporting finished annotations
Click on ':' next to  'Run' button (don't click on run) and select 'Run for project'. A new window will pop up. Select all images and click on 'OK'. Repeat the entire exporting process for each class that you have annotated (so you do it 6 times, running more than one class at once will create multilaballed output and we want to have binaries). Contact the person responsible for your files when you're done with all images

All-in-one metadata storage
🔹 Labels Metadata – Annotates segmented regions or classifications.🔹 Multiscale Metadata – Stores images at multiple resolutions for efficient visualization.🔹 Coordinate Transformations – Maps pixel data to physical space for accurate measurements.🔹 Well Metadata – Captures plate-based experiment details (e.g., well positions, conditions).🔹 Image Acquisition Metadata – Records imaging settings (e.g., exposure time, instrument used).🔹 Experiment Metadata – Stores experiment details (e.g., sample descriptions, protocols).

source_file: PRESENTATIONS_and_POSTERS/Conferences & workshops/European Society of Spatial Biology 2024.docx
text:
European Society of Spatial Biology 2024
Conference overview and suggestions for the future
Location 
Berlin, Germany (December 2024)
Speakers
Big guys in the field were there (eg. Peter Sorger, Bern Bodenmiller, Julio Saez Rodriguez*)
Quite diverse backgrounds (proteomics, transcriptomics etc.)
Julio is a nice spanish guy that now is the Head of Research at the European Bioinformatics Institute, he has developed various methods into omics integration.
Topics covered
Especially packages and some experimental techniques
Case studies demonstrating the practical impact of spatial approaches in various biological systems
Poster sessions
Poorly organized poster sessions
Method- or tool-centered posters were attracting the attention
Biologically centered posters were not so successful
Overall organization – this was the first edition
Venue and catering were nice, and it was easy to find a suitable accommodation
2-day conference, with very dense days full of talks
Limited interaction and networking opportunities between participants, not enough time
Talks
Resolving the tissue ecosystem into its components by Joakim Lundeberg, Sweden
He highlighted that the ecosystem of cells is critical for correct cell typing.
Tools for bioinformatic analysis of spatial transcriptomics:
Semla – R package for Spatially Resolved Transcriptomics Analysis (https://github.com/ludvigla/semla)
Deep generative model for higher spatial resolution which integrates spatial gene expression data with histological image data (https://doi.org/10.1038/s41587-021-01075-3)
FICTURE - segmentation-free spatial factorization method for spatial transcriptomics (https://doi.org/10.1101/2023.11.04.565621)

## Slide 2
Tumor genotypes shape the tumor microenvironment (TME) and 
responses to immunotherapies in high-grade serous cancer (HGSC)
HGSC is the most common and most lethal of ovarian cancers 
50% of the tumors are deficient in Homologous recombination (HR) DNA repair
Tumor genotypes shape the TME and responses to immuno- and combination therapies (Zhang 2018, Iyer 2020, Färkkilä 2021, Launonen 2022)
(Perez-Villatoro, https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.1101/2021.08.19.456809v2)
Speaker notes: HGSC is the most common and lethal of ovarian cancers. 50% of the genomic alterations in high-grade serous ovarian cancers lead to deficiency in homologous recombination DNA repair. 
There is increaseing evidence that these genomic alterations also shape the tumor microenvironment. Zhang et al showed evidence of prognostic interactions of tumor genotypes and immune system. 
And recently we showed that the tumor genotypes associated with distinct TMEs and responses to immunotherapies.

## Slide 3
Precision medicine platform for immuno-oncology in HGSC
Speaker notes: To enable precision medicine in HGSC, we have developed an innovative platform of short-term immuno-competent patient-derived culture to functionally test patient-specific responses to novel immuno- and combination therapies.

Title: Actionable spatial prostanoid barriers constrain BiTE-driven adoptive T cell immunity in intact human tumors 
Author: Dr. Bovannak S. Chap et al.
Reviewer: Anniina Färkkilä, Kürşat Birgin, Joonas Jukonen 
Date: 
Chap et al. investigate adoptive T-cell therapy responses using patient-derived explants from NSCLC and HGSOC, where mesothelin-targeting BiTE-secreting T cells are tested within intact tumor microenvironments. They report heterogeneous, antigen-dependent activity, with resistance associated with low antigen density and stromal and myeloid suppressive states, and identify COX/PGE2 signaling as a modifiable constraint on T-cell function. Overall, the study provides a translational framework for dissecting microenvironmental determinants of ACT efficacy, although aspects of causal inference and response definition require further clarification. 
Major Comments:
KB: Strength of causal inference for COX/PGE2 axis remains partially supported
The COX/PGE2 axis is strongly implicated across multiple datasets (Fig. 4–5), including:
correlation between basal PGE2 and CD8⁺CD137⁺ activation (Fig. 4B)
granzyme B suppression (Fig. 4C)
pharmacologic rescue with COXi in NSCLC and HGSOC PDEs (Fig. 4D–F)
spatial enhancement of CD8 infiltration and tumor-restricted apoptosis (Fig. 5C–I)
However, the causal depth remains limited by:
pharmacologic perturbation only (ketorolac, EP2/EP4 inhibitors)
small patient numbers in some intervention arms (notably NSCLC COXi rescue)

of CD8 infiltration and tumor-restricted apoptosis (Fig. 5C–I)
However, the causal depth remains limited by:
pharmacologic perturbation only (ketorolac, EP2/EP4 inhibitors)
small patient numbers in some intervention arms (notably NSCLC COXi rescue)
lack of genetic or orthogonal pathway disruption (COX/PTGES/EP axis not independently validated)
The conclusion that COX/PGE2 is a dominant “tissue-imposed ceiling” is strongly suggested but still not fully causal.
KB: Response stratification framework is central but requires stronger justification
The CD8⁺CD137⁺-based responder definition (Fig. 6A–F) is a key analytical pillar.
Based on Figure 6:
responder classification is primarily driven by CD137 induction fold change
downstream cytokines (GZMB, IFNγ, TNFα) support the grouping (Fig. 6C–E, G–I)
spatial and baseline TME features correlate with response state (Fig. 6J–Q)
However:
the cutoff threshold (<2-fold vs ≥2-fold) is not justified statistically
no sensitivity analysis is shown for alternate thresholds
classification is heavily anchored on a single marker (CD137), despite availability of multi-parametric outputs
Given that this stratification is used for major biological conclusions (including stromal and myeloid associations), a robustness analysis would strengthen confidence.
KB: PDE selection and exclusion criteria may introduce biological bias
Figure 2 shows a strong filtering pipeline:
69 lesions screened → 24 retained (Fig. 2A, G)
exclusion based on tumor fraction, necrosis, and MSLN expression
While methodologically reasonable, this raises concerns that:
PDEs are enriched for epithelial-rich, MSLN-positive lesions
stromal-rich / immune-poor tumors may be systematically excluded

lusion based on tumor fraction, necrosis, and MSLN expression
While methodologically reasonable, this raises concerns that:
PDEs are enriched for epithelial-rich, MSLN-positive lesions
stromal-rich / immune-poor tumors may be systematically excluded
conclusions about “resistance states” may therefore under-represent extreme immune-desert phenotypes
The manuscript would benefit from clearer comparison between included vs excluded lesions (at least basic histological or clinical characteristics).
KB: Spatial transcriptomics is strong but still largely descriptive
Figure 7 provides convincing spatial separation between responders and non-responders:
responder lesions show IFNγ, antigen presentation, chemokine programs (Fig. 7D–F, I–J)
non-responders show ECM, vascular, CAF, and stress programs (Fig. 7G–H)
However:
spatial segmentation into tumor/stroma/mixed regions is not quantitatively validated with spatial statistics
“immune hub” definition is qualitative rather than formally defined
sample size is limited (n=5 PDEs total), which constrains generalization
The conclusions are plausible but would benefit from more formal spatial quantification (e.g., neighborhood enrichment, spatial autocorrelation metrics).
KB: COX inhibition vs EP2/EP4 blockade interpretation
Figure 4 shows clear differences between:
COXi (ketorolac)
EP2/EP4 receptor inhibition
COXi appears more effective in:
restoring CD8 activation (Fig. 4F)
broadening cytokine networks (Fig. 4J)
shifting myeloid phenotype toward CD68⁺CD80⁺ (Fig. 4K–N)
However:
EP2/EP4 inhibition is not equivalently deep in validation breadth
pharmacologic comparability (dose, exposure, pathway coverage) is not fully established
Thus, the conclusion that upstream COX inhibition is universally superior should be slightly tempered.

inhibition is not equivalently deep in validation breadth
pharmacologic comparability (dose, exposure, pathway coverage) is not fully established
Thus, the conclusion that upstream COX inhibition is universally superior should be slightly tempered.
KB: Infiltration interpretation is well supported but still ex vivo-limited
Figure 5 provides strong volumetric evidence:
true intratumoral CD8⁺ penetration into CK⁺ nests (Fig. 5B–D)
increased penetration with COXi
tumor-restricted apoptosis via TUNEL and CC3 (Fig. 5E–I)
This is one of the strongest parts of the manuscript.
However:
infiltration occurs in a non-perfused system
motility vs retention cannot be fully distinguished from ex vivo imaging alone
interpretation as “in vivo-like recruitment” should be slightly moderated
JJ:
Overall, the there is a need to add/correct legends and color coding explanations in many panels in several figures, including data representations in panels as well as X/Y axis titles. These make the interpretation of the figures difficult; even though the information would be listed in the figure legend, at times the figure legend does not explicitly state e.g. the colors used, so adding the information directly to the panels will improve the legibility of the figures
SFig1A-B: Different tags were used for the MSLN-CAR (Myc) and MSLN-BiTE (FLAG) - why were these specific tags chosen for these constructs? Were different tags tested in addition to these, and were there functional changes to either the CAR or BiTE based on the tag present? Were the constructs tested without tags to see whether the addition of a tag induces functional changes (mainly impairment of function) to the CAR or BiTE?

these, and were there functional changes to either the CAR or BiTE based on the tag present? Were the constructs tested without tags to see whether the addition of a tag induces functional changes (mainly impairment of function) to the CAR or BiTE?
SFig1B displays two distinct CAR population peaks that are used as the comparison for similar transduction efficiencies (BiTE vs CAR) - however the leftmost CAR peak seems uncannily close to the control and the gating still includes a large amount of negative cells. The gating strategy leads to potentially having a smaller successfully transduced CAR population in the experiments, potentially skewing the CAR vs BiTE comparisons due to having less active CAR cells in comparison to BiTE cells per condition. The SFig1B is also missing legends for the coloring of the graphs, reducing legibility
OVCAR-8 is used as the sole cell line model for the initial experiments which are then expanded to HGSC and NSCLC PDX experimentation. Reinforcing cell line assays with another MSLN-expressing cell line (preferrably one that is different from OVCAR-8 in its platinum resistance status), and using a MSLN-low and a MSLN-negative cell line would bring additional to these functional assays and display the specificity of the CAR/BiTE constructs.
Fig1E representation could use an improvement - as the difference between CAR and BiTE appears dramatic, the starting condition for the BiTE-treated sample should be clearly visualized to rule out differences in cell density of starting conditions.
Fig2A: The panel does not explain the qualification conditions for the samples that were retained. The text does not go into the specifics for the inclusion/exclusion criteria for the samples retained for further analysis.

ity of starting conditions.
Fig2A: The panel does not explain the qualification conditions for the samples that were retained. The text does not go into the specifics for the inclusion/exclusion criteria for the samples retained for further analysis. Thus, the selection of samples for downstream analyses cannot be evaluated. The inclusion criteria need to be clearly stated and/or added to the Fig2A schematic.
Fig2D,E: When selecting samples for further assays, it would be translationally prudent to evaluate the MSLN expression of the tissue sample of origin and compare that to the PDE, as cells often rapidly transform during explantation processes, and expression levels of proteins can have large variations in them between in vivo and ex vivo conditions. This would link the finding more directly to the starting state of the cells, and by extension, the patient.
Fig3B-J: The results of the cytokine and T cell activation analyses displaying the BiTE-treated samples have discrepancies - certain NSCLC PDE samples in the panels (e.g. #37, #31) have a low expression of MSLN, yet they have some of the highest cytokine response rates. The cytokine assay results are in turn not strongly correlated with T cell activation levels measured in Fig3B. The samples with strong responses have a major impact on the statistical testing of cytokine assays, but the underlying data implies that the response is affected by more than MSLN expression. I.e.

ted with T cell activation levels measured in Fig3B. The samples with strong responses have a major impact on the statistical testing of cytokine assays, but the underlying data implies that the response is affected by more than MSLN expression. I.e. why do specific samples with high MSLN expression respond weakly, while some with low MSLN expression respond strongly to BiTE treatment? With this in mind, the statements in text (lines 240 - 244) regarding MSLN expression of PDEs being a key determinant of BiTE-mediated T cell activation, and contradictory to the start of the next paragraph starting from line 247.
Fig4D: No justification is given for the selection of the specific two NSCLC PDEs nor are their basal PGE2 concentrations denoted. As the responses have a large variance between the PDEs, the information of the basal PGE2 levels would supplement the shown data. The low sample number in the assay with large intersample variance calls for additional samples to obtain stronger results. Furthermore, no characterization of additional immune cell types present in the TME of the PDEs is performed. This could have a large effect on the cytokine release profiles.
Fig4E: There are two clear PGE2 response types (COXi non-responders vs responders) for COXi in Fig4E, however only the median of the treatment groups is taken into account in the text. Certain samples (#6, #25) appear to even have their PGE2 levels increase upon COXi.
Fig4C,F-I,K-N: There are large inter-PDE differences in COXi responses, T cell activation responses and myeloid-cell based responses. The performed analysis at the PDE level does not take into account the differences in responses between PDEs and would need further investigation to clarify the entire spectrum of PDE subset responses

tion responses and myeloid-cell based responses. The performed analysis at the PDE level does not take into account the differences in responses between PDEs and would need further investigation to clarify the entire spectrum of PDE subset responses
Fig5B-D: The shown data does not correlate with statements in text. The lymphocyte infiltration measured by CD8 positivity does not strongly correlate with cytokeratin staining in Fig5B, nor is it possible to see the correlation between boxplots in Fig5C-D and the volumetric masked images next to the box plots. Especially the image resolution is lacking, and the nuclear stain intensity too low.
Fig5F-I: It is unclear how many samples constitute each violin plot, and whether the analysis was done on a sample level, or at the volumetric tumor region level. Violin plot necessarily is not the best visualization for this data, but could be improved by e.g. box plot visualization with individual data points displayed. Furthermore, having only cytokeratin positivity as the phenotyping marker in the assay does not enable the evaluation of cell type specific off-tumor effects. There seem to be data points with high non-tumor TUNEL-positivity (Fig5G), are those correlated with sample PDE tumor cell MSLN positivity? Why was EP2/4i left out as a control in Fig5H-I?
Fig6F-I: EP2/4i-treatment reduces immune activation marker levels in the responders across the panels, yet the potential causes behind this phenomenon, which is observed only here and not in other experiments involving EP2/4i, is not discussed at all. Additional consideration for this phenomenon would strengthen the analysis. Additionally, panels G-I lack the UT control setting.

nd this phenomenon, which is observed only here and not in other experiments involving EP2/4i, is not discussed at all. Additional consideration for this phenomenon would strengthen the analysis. Additionally, panels G-I lack the UT control setting.
Fig6J-Q: Panels J-K are missing the patient legend, panels L-Q are missing the patient-specific color coding entirely, leading to .
Fig7: Panel D could be improved by highlighting the important findings within the heatmap - the heatmap contains a massive amount of information, and the visualization does not offer any guides regarding the sections mentioned in the text. Fig7D-E panel image quality/resolution is also low, increasing the difficulty of result interpretation.
Fig7G-J: the visual connection between single-transcript level volcano plots and the enrichment analysis is very unclear. Additionally, why was only GO used in panel H, and only Reactome in panel J? If using both in the panels, it would be useful to see the output of both databases, since they contain very different levels of data.
Minor Comments:
1.) KB: COX inhibitor specificity
Ketorolac is appropriately used but potential off-target anti-inflammatory effects should be explicitly acknowledged in discussion.
JJ:
Fig1C: missing the panel header letter
FigC, D, F: missing the legend for the color coding of the bar plots
SFig 2C: Y axis header mislabeled (% of immune population), as the data has several cell types characterized
SFig2D: No information given on what PDE section is in question - HGSC or NSCLC, sample number, nor is scale bar size informed in the figure panel

C: Y axis header mislabeled (% of immune population), as the data has several cell types characterized
SFig2D: No information given on what PDE section is in question - HGSC or NSCLC, sample number, nor is scale bar size informed in the figure panel
Fig4E: visualization of individual sample colors within bar plot is unclear and could be improved with slightly larger individual data point size, and avoiding using very similar shades of blue/green
Fig4F-I, Fig4K-N: add a sample legend, or otherwise improve clarity to inform the reader that the PDEs in use in these panels are HGSC and not NSCLC, and for the reader to be able to identify the individual samples involved in the assays
Volumetric imaging assay: main text indicates BiTE cells to be “Celltrace Far Red labeled” (line 339), however the methods indicate Celltrace yellow for both BiTE and UT T cells. No manufacturer is detailed for the reagents.
To the editor:
This is a high-quality, multi-modal manuscript that combines functional immunology, spatial transcriptomics, and patient-derived explant systems to study ACT responses in solid tumors. The PDE platform is particularly strong, and the combination of functional, spatial, and mechanistic datasets is a major strength.
The most convincing aspects of the work include:
robust antigen-dependent BiTE responses in intact human tissue (Fig. 3)
volumetric demonstration of tumor-restricted T cell infiltration and killing (Fig. 5)
spatially resolved immune hub formation in responder lesions (Fig. 7)
However, the central mechanistic claim regarding COX/PGE2 as a dominant and broadly targetable resistance axis would benefit from deeper causal validation and more explicit acknowledgment of pharmacologic limitations.

on in responder lesions (Fig. 7)
However, the central mechanistic claim regarding COX/PGE2 as a dominant and broadly targetable resistance axis would benefit from deeper causal validation and more explicit acknowledgment of pharmacologic limitations. Additionally, the responder classification framework, while biologically intuitive, requires stronger statistical justification.
Overall, the manuscript is likely to be of high interest to the immunotherapy and tumor microenvironment fields, but would benefit from moderate revision to strengthen causal inference and analytical rigor.

text:
I thank the authors for the added nuclei panels, however these present new questions regarding the cell states in the model. In Figure 1B and 1D, the perfusion model images both contain nuclei with far larger sizes than their controls. Similar observations can be made from the slow frozen samples in comparison to their controls in Figures 2A, 2D, 3A, 3B. 
The numerous links between nucleus size increase and senescence exist in literature, the question arises if the strikingly enlarged nuclei are due to senescence (i.e. that instead of imminent cell death, senescence-related cellular programs are activated).
To address this, it would be extremely important to show that a major fraction of cancer cells are not senescent in the models depicted, as the cell behaviour changes radically upon senescence, and a majorly senescent cancer cell population would not be an accurate reflection of the microenvironment.
To the editor:
I feel that the revisions submitted by the authors are mostly acceptable, however the quality of the representative immunofluorescence images are still not markedly improved. I feel that the senescence is an important issue that should definitely be addressed before considering the manuscript for acceptance.

Consolidated comments: 
Song et al. present paroxetine, an FDA-approved antidepressant, as a potential molecular glue degrader of CDH6 through recruitment of TRIM21. The authors propose that paroxetine binding to CDH6 promotes CDH6 degradation and thereby suppresses aggressive ovarian cancer phenotypes in vitro and in vivo. The study addresses an interesting therapeutic concept, particularly given the relevance of CDH6 in ovarian cancer and the broader interest in molecular glue degraders.
However, while parts of the biochemical and biophysical characterization are potentially interesting, the manuscript in its current form does not provide sufficiently rigorous or transparent evidence to support several of the central functional and therapeutic claims. The description of experimental conditions is often incomplete, several key controls are missing, statistical analysis is insufficiently justified, and important discrepancies between the reported functional data and prior literature are not adequately addressed. These issues make it difficult to assess the robustness, reproducibility, and biological relevance of the findings.
In particular, the functional assays require substantial strengthening. The data supporting paroxetine-induced cytotoxicity are not fully consistent across assay types, the comparison with standard therapies is not sufficiently rigorous, and the use of ovarian cancer models requires stronger justification. Moreover, mechanistic target engagement in vivo remains largely inferred. The manuscript would benefit from clearer framing, more complete methodological reporting, additional validation experiments, and a more cautious interpretation of the data.
Major comments
1.

nistic target engagement in vivo remains largely inferred. The manuscript would benefit from clearer framing, more complete methodological reporting, additional validation experiments, and a more cautious interpretation of the data.
Major comments
1. The rationale for selecting paroxetine from the antidepressant screen is insufficiently developed
The manuscript does not clearly explain why antidepressant drugs were screened in the first place. This motivation should be introduced more explicitly and linked to a testable biological or therapeutic hypothesis.
In addition, the screen appears to have identified several antidepressants with activity, yet only paroxetine was selected for detailed validation. The authors should clarify why paroxetine was prioritized over the other active compounds. Was paroxetine the most potent compound, the most selective compound, the compound with the strongest CDH6 dependency, or selected for another reason?
To support specificity, the authors should provide validation data for at least some of the other active antidepressants identified in the screen. This is particularly important to determine whether the observed effects are specific to paroxetine or reflect a broader property of this drug class.
2. Specificity of CDH6 as the relevant paroxetine target requires stronger support
The authors identify CDH6 as a paroxetine-binding protein and propose that CDH6 degradation mediates the antitumor effects of paroxetine. However, the specificity of CDH6 as the relevant functional target of paroxetine is not fully established.
The manuscript should more clearly address whether CDH6 is a selective target of paroxetine or whether paroxetine interacts with other proteins that may contribute to the observed phenotypes.

functional target of paroxetine is not fully established.
The manuscript should more clearly address whether CDH6 is a selective target of paroxetine or whether paroxetine interacts with other proteins that may contribute to the observed phenotypes. Additional rescue, knockdown, or CDH6-low/CDH6-negative model experiments would help clarify the degree to which the antitumor effects are CDH6-dependent.
The TCGA expression and survival association analyses currently included around lines 196–202 are not clearly connected to the discovery of paroxetine as a CDH6-targeting compound. The authors should better justify the relevance of these analyses to the central mechanistic claim, or consider moving them to a later section where the disease relevance of CDH6 is discussed. Validation in an additional disease context or model system would also strengthen the claim that this mechanism is relevant beyond the specific models used.
3. Use of ovarian cancer cell lines requires clearer justification and broader validation
OVCAR3 and SKOV3 are widely used ovarian cancer cell lines, but both have important biological caveats. The manuscript initially uses both cell lines, but later mechanistic sections rely predominantly on OVCAR3 cells, without a clear rationale. The authors should explain why SKOV3 was omitted from later experiments or provide key mechanistic validation in SKOV3 as well.
The use of SKOV3 as a high-grade serous ovarian cancer model is problematic, as SKOV3 is atypical for this disease context, lacks canonical TP53 mutation, and is generally considered homologous recombination proficient. The authors should acknowledge this limitation and avoid overgeneralizing from this model.

is problematic, as SKOV3 is atypical for this disease context, lacks canonical TP53 mutation, and is generally considered homologous recombination proficient. The authors should acknowledge this limitation and avoid overgeneralizing from this model.
The manuscript would be strengthened by including an additional, more representative ovarian cancer model, ideally a BRCA-mutant or otherwise clearly homologous recombination-deficient high-grade serous ovarian cancer model, especially given the use of olaparib as a comparator.
4. Reported olaparib responses, particularly in SKOV3, are unexpected and require explanation
The reported olaparib responses in SKOV3 and OVCAR3 appear surprisingly similar, despite the very different homologous recombination characteristics of these models. This is difficult to reconcile with prior literature indicating that SKOV3 cells are relatively resistant to olaparib, with 10 µM olaparib often insufficient to elicit strong responses.
The authors should explain how their olaparib response data align with the known biology of these cell lines. They should also justify the use of olaparib in an HR-proficient SKOV3 model and provide additional information on culture conditions, treatment duration, drug source, and assay readout.
At present, the olaparib data raise concerns about either the biological interpretation or the experimental setup. Additional controls, ideally including a known olaparib-sensitive BRCA-mutant model, would be important.
5. Comparisons with existing therapies are not sufficiently rigorous
The authors compare paroxetine with olaparib and cisplatin, but statistical comparisons appear to be performed mainly against vehicle controls rather than directly between treatment groups.

risons with existing therapies are not sufficiently rigorous
The authors compare paroxetine with olaparib and cisplatin, but statistical comparisons appear to be performed mainly against vehicle controls rather than directly between treatment groups. As a result, it is difficult to determine whether paroxetine performs significantly differently from existing therapeutic options.
The authors should include appropriate statistical comparisons between paroxetine, olaparib, cisplatin, and combination or control groups where relevant. The current data may support paroxetine as a potentially active compound, but they do not yet establish that it provides superior or clinically meaningful benefit relative to established therapies.
6. Functional viability and cell death assays are internally inconsistent
The cell viability data in Fig. 1b suggest an IC50 of approximately 10 µM for both OVCAR3 and SKOV3. However, the flow cytometry data in Fig. 1g show limited evidence of definitive cell death. In SKOV3, PI positivity appears to reach only approximately 10%, while in OVCAR3 PI positivity is minimal. Annexin V positivity alone may reflect cellular stress or early apoptosis, but without substantial PI positivity or additional apoptosis markers, the data do not convincingly demonstrate robust cell death.
This discrepancy should be addressed directly. CCK-8 primarily measures metabolic activity and is not, by itself, a definitive measure of cell viability or cell death. The authors should include additional orthogonal assays, such as live-cell imaging, caspase activation, cleaved PARP, cleaved caspase-3, colony formation, or direct cell counting, to clarify whether paroxetine causes cytotoxicity, cytostasis, metabolic suppression, or another cellular state.
7.

nal assays, such as live-cell imaging, caspase activation, cleaved PARP, cleaved caspase-3, colony formation, or direct cell counting, to clarify whether paroxetine causes cytotoxicity, cytostasis, metabolic suppression, or another cellular state.
7. In vivo models and interpretation require strengthening
The in vivo experiments show dose-dependent reduction in tumor volume and tumor weight following paroxetine treatment, but several issues limit interpretation.
First, the xenograft experiments were performed using subcutaneous implantation. For ovarian cancer, intraperitoneal models are often more biologically relevant, particularly when studying tumor dissemination and peritoneal disease burden. The authors should justify the use of subcutaneous models and discuss the limitations.
Second, there appears to be substantial variation in the vehicle group in Fig. 1k, approaching an approximately five-fold range. This raises concerns about the robustness of the treatment effect and should be addressed through clearer reporting of sample size, randomization, exclusion criteria, and statistical analysis.
Third, in vivo mechanistic target engagement remains inferred. The authors should provide direct evidence that paroxetine reduces CDH6 protein levels in tumor tissues. Immunohistochemistry or immunoblotting of treatment-naive and paroxetine-treated xenograft tumors for CDH6 would substantially strengthen the study. Additional markers of downstream pathway suppression and apoptosis, such as cleaved caspase-3, would also be useful.
8. DARTS assay interpretation should be stated more cautiously
The authors state that the DARTS assay allowed assessment of ligand-induced stabilization of proteins against proteolytic degradation in native biological systems.

, would also be useful.
8. DARTS assay interpretation should be stated more cautiously
The authors state that the DARTS assay allowed assessment of ligand-induced stabilization of proteins against proteolytic degradation in native biological systems. However, based on the described setup, ligand binding appears to have been assessed in cell lysates exposed ex vivo to paroxetine rather than in intact cells.
The wording should be revised to reflect this distinction. The assay supports ligand-induced stabilization under controlled lysate conditions, but does not by itself demonstrate intracellular target engagement under physiological cellular conditions.
In addition, the authors state that paroxetine-binding hits were first identified through a 90 kDa protein band that increased in a dose-dependent manner in paroxetine-treated lysates. However, the SDS-PAGE image in Extended Data Fig. 3a does not clearly support this claim. A clearer gel image and quantitative densitometry should be provided.
A full negative control lacking both pronase and paroxetine also appears to be missing, making it difficult to assess baseline protein abundance and interpret the DARTS result confidently.
9. Recombinant CDH6 domain characterization is insufficient
The manuscript does not provide adequate characterization of the recombinant CDH6 extracellular and intracellular domains used in the biophysical assays. The authors should specify the exact amino acid boundaries of the constructs used and describe how the proteins were expressed, purified, and validated.
For the extracellular domain, it is particularly important to demonstrate that the recombinant protein was properly folded before MST or other binding assays.

the constructs used and describe how the proteins were expressed, purified, and validated.
For the extracellular domain, it is particularly important to demonstrate that the recombinant protein was properly folded before MST or other binding assays. Misfolded domains may fail to interact with ligands or may generate misleading binding results.
10. Western blot data need quantification and full reporting of replicates
The manuscript states that most Western blots are representative of three independent replicates, but only representative blots are shown. This leaves two-thirds of the data unavailable to the reader and makes it difficult to assess the robustness of the claims.
The authors should provide quantification for all Western blot experiments, including individual data points from biological replicates, normalization strategy, statistical testing, and description of the quantification method. This is particularly important for claims related to CDH6 degradation, TRIM21 dependency, pathway suppression, and rescue experiments.
11. Computational modeling results should be framed with appropriate limitations
The computational model is supported by several orthogonal experimental approaches, including biophysical measurements, mutational analyses, and functional validation using chemical analogs. However, the simulations were performed using a truncated 38-amino-acid CDH6 peptide centered on the proposed degron region rather than the full intracellular domain.
The authors should explicitly state this limitation in the Results section.

nalogs. However, the simulations were performed using a truncated 38-amino-acid CDH6 peptide centered on the proposed degron region rather than the full intracellular domain.
The authors should explicitly state this limitation in the Results section. Although the validation data support the functional relevance of the predicted interaction interface, additional contacts outside the modeled peptide may contribute to ternary complex formation, stabilization, or degradation efficiency in cells.
12. Statistical analysis is inadequately described and may be inappropriate
The manuscript uses parametric statistical tests, but there is no indication that the authors assessed whether the data met the assumptions of these tests, including normal distribution and variance homogeneity. This is particularly concerning given the small sample sizes typical of many of the experiments.
The authors should clarify how statistical assumptions were assessed. If assumptions were not met or sample sizes are too small to justify parametric testing, appropriate non-parametric tests should be used. The manuscript should also clearly define biological versus technical replicates, report exact n values, and specify how multiple comparisons were handled.
13. Experimental conditions are insufficiently described and limit reproducibility
Many methodological descriptions are too vague to allow replication. The authors should provide substantially more detail throughout the Methods.
Examples include:
OVCAR3 culture conditions are described as RPMI-1640 with 20% FBS. This is atypical and requires justification. The absence of insulin and glutamine supplementation should also be clarified.

ally more detail throughout the Methods.
Examples include:
OVCAR3 culture conditions are described as RPMI-1640 with 20% FBS. This is atypical and requires justification. The absence of insulin and glutamine supplementation should also be clarified.
Antibody dilutions should be reported separately for each application, including Western blotting, immunoprecipitation, and immunofluorescence.
Immunoprecipitation conditions are insufficiently described.
The duration of cell culture before immunofluorescence treatments is not stated.
Western blot quantification methods are not described.
Drug treatment conditions, cell density, passage number, and timing should be reported more completely.
Without these details, the experiments are difficult to reproduce and evaluate.
14. Immunofluorescence experiments may not reflect the conditions used in functional assays
The representative immunofluorescence images show sparse clusters of approximately four to six cells with large empty areas, suggesting very low cell density at the time of analysis. These conditions appear substantially different from the likely conditions used in functional assays and may influence cell behavior, drug response, protein localization, and pathway activity.
The authors should clarify seeding densities and culture duration for immunofluorescence experiments and explain whether these conditions are comparable to those used in viability, degradation, and functional assays.
15. The Introduction is too long and insufficiently focused
The Introduction is lengthy and reads partly like a Discussion. It also appears to describe some of the study findings before clearly establishing the prior knowledge gap.

functional assays.
15. The Introduction is too long and insufficiently focused
The Introduction is lengthy and reads partly like a Discussion. It also appears to describe some of the study findings before clearly establishing the prior knowledge gap. This makes it difficult to distinguish what was already known from what is newly shown in the present manuscript.
The authors should shorten and refocus the Introduction. It should clearly establish:
the biological and clinical relevance of CDH6,
the rationale for seeking CDH6-targeting strategies,
the conceptual basis for drug repurposing or antidepressant screening, and
the specific knowledge gap addressed by this study.
Detailed interpretation of the current findings should be moved to the Discussion.

Fig. 1g: The interpretation of Annexin V and PI staining should be described more cautiously. Annexin V positivity without PI positivity does not necessarily establish definitive cell death.

Drug naming: The manuscript should consistently use either “paroxetine” or “PXT” after defining the abbreviation. Similarly, cisplatin, olaparib, and other drug names should be used consistently.
Cell culture conditions: The use of 20% FBS for OVCAR3 culture should be justified, and the absence or presence of insulin and glutamine supplementation should be explicitly stated.

Statistical reporting: Exact n values, test names, definitions of error bars, and multiple-testing corrections should be reported in each figure legend.
