Model terminology: The term “PDX” should only be used if patient-derived xenografts were used. If the experiments used cell-line-derived xenografts, the terminology should be corrected.
Confidential comments to editor: 
This manuscript presents an interesting concept: that paroxetine may act as a molecular glue degrader of CDH6 through TRIM21 recruitment. The biochemical and biophysical parts of the study are the strongest components and provide some support for an interaction between paroxetine, CDH6, and TRIM21. However, even in these sections, some claims should be toned down to better reflect the limitations of the assays performed, particularly regarding intracellular target engagement and the use of truncated CDH6 constructs in modeling experiments.
Our major concern is that the functional and therapeutic claims are not currently supported with sufficient rigor.

ays performed, particularly regarding intracellular target engagement and the use of truncated CDH6 constructs in modeling experiments.
Our major concern is that the functional and therapeutic claims are not currently supported with sufficient rigor. The functional assays contain important methodological weaknesses, incomplete descriptions, and internal inconsistencies. The CCK-8 viability data and flow cytometry cell death data do not align well, making it unclear whether paroxetine induces true cytotoxicity, cytostasis, metabolic suppression, or another phenotype. The in vivo experiments rely on subcutaneous xenografts, which are not the most relevant model for ovarian cancer dissemination, and there is substantial variability in the vehicle group. Direct evidence of CDH6 degradation in treated tumor tissue is lacking.
There are also significant concerns regarding the model systems and comparator treatments. The reported olaparib sensitivity of SKOV3 is unexpected given its homologous recombination-proficient status and appears inconsistent with prior literature. The similarity of olaparib responses between SKOV3 and OVCAR3 raises concerns about the experimental setup or interpretation. OVCAR3 culture conditions are also atypical, including use of 20% FBS and unclear supplementation. The manuscript does not provide sufficient methodological detail to allow confident assessment or replication of several key experiments.
Overall, while the molecular mechanism proposed is potentially interesting, the manuscript in its current form overstates the therapeutic implications and does not provide sufficiently robust functional evidence to support publication in Nature Cancer.

erall, while the molecular mechanism proposed is potentially interesting, the manuscript in its current form overstates the therapeutic implications and does not provide sufficiently robust functional evidence to support publication in Nature Cancer. Substantial additional validation, improved methodological transparency, more appropriate statistical analysis, and a more cautious framing of the conclusions would be required.
Title: Paroxetine acts as a molecular glue degrader of CDH6 via TRIM21 recruitment: a new therapeutic strategy for oncogenic cadherin driven diseases
Tracking: NATCANCER-A23777
Author: Lingyi Song et al.
Reviewer: Anniina Färkkilä, María Hincapié-Otero, Joonas Jukonen 
Date: 
General description:
Song et al present an FDA-approved compound to be able to bind to CDH6 of ovarian cancer cells, and direct it to proteolytic degradation, hindering ovarian cancer aggressiveness in both in vitro and in vivo models. However, the presentation of the work performed is lacking critical details including for controls and experimental setup. Additionally, there are questions raised by the discrepancies between the results of functional studies in comparison to previous work in the literature, especially on the PARPi responses of one cell line. Due to the gaps in the information provided in the manuscript, it is difficult to place trust in the work presented and accept the claims made by the authors.
The introduction is lengthy, unfocused, and written more in the style of discussion. Introduction should not broadly describe the study findings, now it is unclear which parts are known before, and what is done in the present study. 
The motivation of screening antidepressant drugs is not clear.

ten more in the style of discussion. Introduction should not broadly describe the study findings, now it is unclear which parts are known before, and what is done in the present study. 
The motivation of screening antidepressant drugs is not clear. 
From the screen - there were at least 5 other antidepressants that showed efficacy. Why was paroxetine selected only for validation? Was the effect in vitro and/or in vivo specific to paroxetine? To show specificity, validation data should be presented on the other drugs effective in the screen.
What about the specificity of CDH6 as the target of paroxetin - is that specific to paroxetin?
Rows - 196-202: Expression in TCGA or survival associations - how are these relevant to the discovery of paroxetin target protein? This should be validated in another disease context- or model system. 
Major Comments:
MM: 
OVCAR3 and SKOV3 cell lines are widely used in ovarian cancer research. However, some concerns about this arise in this paper: 
In the first part of the manuscript experiments were replicated in both cell lines. However, in later sections only OVCAR3 cells are used. No clear explanation regarding this selection is presented whatsoever. Please clarify what is the rationale behind this, or provide data supporting similar findings in key mechanistic assays for SKOV3 cells as well.
The olaparib response, particularly in the SKOV3 model, appears somewhat unexpected. Please elaborate on this. 
It is difficult to assess whether PXT performs significantly differently than olaparib or cisplatin, as statistical comparisons appear to have been performed mainly against vehicle controls, and responses to chemotherapy on these two models are quite different.

cult to assess whether PXT performs significantly differently than olaparib or cisplatin, as statistical comparisons appear to have been performed mainly against vehicle controls, and responses to chemotherapy on these two models are quite different. While this data supports to some extent PXT as a promising therapeutic alternative, it remains unclear whether it provides a superior benefit relative to existing treatment options.
Authors claim that DARTS assay allowed them to assess ligand-induced stabilization of proteins against proteolytic degradation in native biological systems (see lines 188 - 191). However, in their setup, ligand binding seems to be assessed in cell lysates exposed ex vivo to PXT, not in intact cells.Therefore, binding occurs under controlled experimental conditions rather than physiological intracellular environments. Please, adjust the wording accordingly.
Authors claim that PXT-binding hits were first identified via a 90 kDa protein band that was upregulated in PXT-treated cell lysates in a dose-dependent manner (see lines 192 - 194). However, the protein gel presented in Extended data Fig 3a does not clearly show this. Please, provide a clearer SDS-page image, preferably with quantified data supporting the claim.
Authors do not show any characterization of the recombinant CDH6 ECD and ICD. Was the ECD properly folded prior to MST assays? Missfolded domains might fail to interact with certain ligands. 
Authors claim that most of the Western blots presented in the manuscript were representative of n = 3 independent replicates/plot. However, as only representative blots are presented, two thirds of the data is missing making it difficult to assess the validity and robustness of the claims.

lots presented in the manuscript were representative of n = 3 independent replicates/plot. However, as only representative blots are presented, two thirds of the data is missing making it difficult to assess the validity and robustness of the claims. Please, include quantifications showing all the data points for the blots. 
While the biochemical, mutational, and ternary complex data strongly support a model in which PXT directly engages CDH6 and promotes TRIM21-mediated degradation, direct intracellular target engagement in vivo remains inferred. The PDX models demonstrate a dose-dependent reduction in tumor volume and weight following PXT treatment; however, additional in vivo evidence of CDH6 degradation and downstream pathway suppression within tumor tissues would further strengthen the mechanistic link between target engagement and antitumor activity. In this regard, immunohistochemical analysis of treatment-naive and PXT-treated xenograft tumors showing CDH6 expression levels and/or apoptosis markers eg. cleaved caspase-3 would be a valuable addition. 
Although the computational model is supported by orthogonal biophysical measurements, mutational analyses, and orthogonal validation via functional assays with chemical analogs, it remains important to emphasize that the simulations were performed using a truncated 38-amino-acid CDH6 peptide centered on the degron region rather than the full intracellular domain. The experimental validations strongly support the functional relevance of the predicted interaction interface; however, additional contacts or interactions outside the modeled region may still contribute to ternary complex formation and stabilization in vivo, and this is not stated in the results section.

To the editor: 
JJ: 
The biochemical part of the manuscript does seem convincing regarding the interactions of PXT with CDH6 and TRIM21. 
However, the functional assays and their methodology are direly lacking, especially containing critical flaws in the methodology and descriptions of the functional experiments as well as their analysis. These bring doubt to the entire functional assays presented. As examples; in vivo experiments are performed using subcutaneous injections instead of intraperitoneal, and cell culture conditions for OVCAR3 used in the experiments are very abnormal. Statistical testing is not performed correctly. Olaparib responses of SKOV3 are contradictory to literature, and shockingly similar to the responses of OVCAR3. The functional assay descriptions in this manuscript are not up to par, the experiment descriptions are lacking a number of important details.

Summaryoftheresearchplan(max. 1000characters)High-grade serous ovarian cancer (HGSC) presents an aggressive growth and poor prognosis, withstandard treatments often falling short in efficacy. In this context, understanding the impact ofsurgery and chemotherapy onthespatial tumor microenvironment (TME) andimmunedynamics isessential. I aimto delineate treatment-associated spatial patterns within theTMEby identifyingrelevant cell neighborhoods andexploring their molecular profiles. Therefore, I will examinea setof patient-derivedsamples fromtheOncosys-Ova study asapartofiCANbyemployingcutting-edgemethods. RESEARCH PLAN 
Applicant´s name: Ada, Junquera Mencía
Start date of study right: 1/1/2024
Title of the doctoral thesis (preliminary): Discovery of spatial vulnerabilities in the tumor microenvironment for precision oncology in ovarian cancer 
Background and aims of the research project
Summary of the research plan (max. 1000 characters)
High-grade serous ovarian cancer (HGSC) presents an aggressive growth and poor prognosis, with standard treatments often falling short in efficacy. In this context, understanding the impact of surgery and chemotherapy on the spatial tumor microenvironment (TME) and immune dynamics is essential. I aim to delineate treatment-associated spatial patterns within the TME by identifying relevant cell neighborhoods and exploring their molecular profiles. Therefore, I will examine a set of patient-derived samples from the Oncosys-Ova study as a part of iCAN by employing cutting-edge methods. These include single-cell spatial profiling through tissue cyclic immunofluorescence (tCycIF) and spatial transcriptomics (GeoMx), coupled with advanced bioinformatic analysis and correlations with extensive clinicopathological data. Ultimately, this thesis will lead to the discovery of novel biomarkers for improved patient stratification and precision oncology.
Rationale for the research project (max. 2000 characters with spaces)
HGSC is the most common and lethal subtype of ovarian cancer. With a 70% relapse rate and a 10-year survival rate of 10%, there is a pressing need for novel HGSC therapeutic strategies. Whilst targeted therapies like PARP inhibitors have shown promise in HGSC patient subsets (Fong et al., 2010) , immune checkpoint inhibition (ICI) yielded disappointing outcomes (Matulonis et al., 2019).

ng need for novel HGSC therapeutic strategies. Whilst targeted therapies like PARP inhibitors have shown promise in HGSC patient subsets (Fong et al., 2010) , immune checkpoint inhibition (ICI) yielded disappointing outcomes (Matulonis et al., 2019). The spatial study of the TME is vital to understand ICI response and find predictive biomarkers (Färkkilä et al., 2020). Although adaptive immunity is implicated in anti-tumor response, its effects in HGSC are poorly characterized. In this regard, I contributed to a study delving into tertiary lymphoid structures (TLSs) (Sarkkinen, Junquera et al, BiorXIV). My work enabled the recognition of TLS-associated gene expression profiles within the TME, characterized by elevated levels of the chemokines CCL19, CCL21, and CXCL13 correlating with the number of TLSs in the images (Fig 1.A). In this study, we also profiled TLSs with tCycIF (Fig. 1B), revealing spatiotemporal gradients of Germinal center-B cell interactions during TLS maturation (Fig 1C). 
Current therapies also exploit tumor-intrinsic vulnerabilities like cell proliferation, arrest and DNA repair mechanisms (DDR), which intersect within the cell cycle. Despite advancements in spatial mapping of proliferation (Gaglia et al., 2022), spatial topographies of the cell cycle in HGSC remain unknown. Investigating this field holds the translational potential to guide therapeutic combinations and patient stratification approaches. In my prior work, I was able to spatially characterize proliferative and arrested cells through t-CycIF (Fig 2.A).

wn. Investigating this field holds the translational potential to guide therapeutic combinations and patient stratification approaches. In my prior work, I was able to spatially characterize proliferative and arrested cells through t-CycIF (Fig 2.A). Moreover, the cell-cycle resolved clustering associated arrested phenotypes of cancer, CD8+ and CD4+ T cells with longer progression free survival (PFS), highlighting the strong relevance of these cell states in clinical outcomes (Fig 2.B).

Research methods and materials to be used and its significance for the research project (max. 3000 characters with spaces) 
Patient-derived samples collected through the ONCOSYS-Ova prospective clinical study conducted as part of iCAN by Helsinki University Hospital and Helsinki University. The study has been ongoing since 2019, recruiting >400 patients. Each patient's contribution involves the collection of fresh and paraffin-embedded (FFPE) samples from primary tumor tissue and/or metastasis. Bulk RNA-seq and whole exome sequencing (WES), BRCA1/2 mutational profiling and genomic scarring analysis (ovaHRDscar) are also available, together with detailed surgical, pathological and clinical data 
t-CycIF multiplexed imaging (Lin et al., 2018) will be employed to perform spatial single-cell profiling of FFPE samples. This method is based on a cyclic immunofluorescence process, ultimately allowing the quantification of >80 antigens per sample. I will:
Optimize novel antibody panels incorporating cell profiling and functional targets
Perform the experiments in a highly reliable Rarecyte Cytefinder II instrument, and process the images through computational platforms including the MCMICRO pipeline and Stardist
Phenotype the single cells by employing TRIBUS and spatially analyze the topographies through the usage of specialized Python packages, such as scimap
Correlate the findings with the clinical data from ONCOSYS-Ova, as well as with pathological annotations in H&E stainings

Nanostring GeoMx spatial transcriptomics will be then utilized to examine the molecular mechanisms operating within these neighborhoods, allowing the identification of gene-expression signatures, pathways and novel biomarkers linked with prognosis and treatment response.
This method allows to profile the mRNA within regions of interest (ROIs) in the tissue containing 20-300 cells each. I will:
Select the ROIs in adjacent FFPE slides to those dedicated for tCycIF (3-5um distance) precisely targeting the relevant cells through a self-developed pipeline from my MSc thesis 
Bioinformatic analysis like GSEA on GO terms and MsigDB pathways, obtaining GSVA scores on selected pathways, WGCNA weighted gene correlation network and ligand-receptor interactions analysis
All the necessary resources and infrastructure are in place to complete the project, including patient samples and clinical data, in-house Rarecyte Cytefinder II and GeoMx instruments, all reagents for tCycIF/GeoMx and computational resources, pipelines and data storage
Preliminary plan on the collection, usage, and storage of the research material (max. 1000 characters with spaces) 
Data is collected in the ongoing prospective ONCOSYS-Ova study performed at Helsinki University Hospital (HUH) and Helsinki University. The study entails the collection of fresh tumor tissue, blood samples and clinical information from patients diagnosed with HGSC at the HUH.

ngoing prospective ONCOSYS-Ova study performed at Helsinki University Hospital (HUH) and Helsinki University. The study entails the collection of fresh tumor tissue, blood samples and clinical information from patients diagnosed with HGSC at the HUH. Fresh frozen samples are obtained for DNA/RNA isolation, while formalin-fixed paraffin-embedded (FFPE) blocks are retrieved to analyze tumor-immune interactions in detail. Data is securely stored in the University of Helsinki's data cloud and L-drive, ensuring the protection of sensitive information. Additionally, all clinical and biological data will be entered into a dedicated database (OVCA-Database) on the HUH server, enabling the data to be processed and analyzed in an anonymous manner. The HUH server, accessible via the HUH Datalake within the Helsinki Biobank, functions as a secure repository for patient IDs where authorized personnel retrieve clinical data.
Ethical issues (max.1000 characters with spaces) 
ONCOSYS-Ova study has received ethical approval from Helsinki University Hospital Women’s Hospital and Helsinki Biobank. All participating patients have provided their informed consent for both the study and the acquisition of fresh tumor samples and clinical data. Before consenting, patients were fully informed of the experimental nature of this project. Nonetheless, in instances where crucial findings arise from the conducted experiments that could potentially impact their treatment, the research team retains the option to communicate with the attending physician. Every individual engaged in this prospective study has formally endorsed the HUH confidentiality disclosure statement. RESEARCH PLAN 
Applicant´s name: Aleksandra, Shabanova
Title of the doctoral thesis (preliminary): Spatial multi-omics profiling of immune recognition in Ovarian Cancer 
Background and aims of the research project
Summary of the research plan
High-grade serous ovarian cancer (HGSC) poses significant clinical challenges due to its aggressive nature and poor prognosis. Genetic alterations, particularly in DNA-repair homologous recombination, contribute to distinct molecular phenotypes in HGSC. While major histocompatibility complex (MHC) has been implicated in anti-tumor immunity, its specific role in immune evasion mechanisms in HGSC subtypes remains underexplored.
Our research focuses on elucidating the role of MHC dysregulation in HGSC. Through a multidisciplinary approach involving machine learning analysis of patient tumor samples, spatial characterization of the tumor microenvironment, and cell-cell interaction studies, we aim to uncover molecular programs underlying MHC-dependent immune evasion and tumor progression in HGSC. By identifying potential prognostic markers and therapeutic targets, our study aims to lay the groundwork for personalized treatment approaches and improved management of HGSC patients.
Rationale for the research project 
Tumorigenesis and cancer progression depend on malignant cells' ability to escape the recognition and attack by the host immune system1. HGSC is characterized by Homologous Recombination DNA repair deficiency (HRD) in 50% of the tumors2,3. Emerging evidence suggests that the HGCSs with HRD harbor distinct immune escape mechanisms4. 
Preliminary data & supporting results of the first manuscript.

aracterized by Homologous Recombination DNA repair deficiency (HRD) in 50% of the tumors2,3. Emerging evidence suggests that the HGCSs with HRD harbor distinct immune escape mechanisms4. 
Preliminary data & supporting results of the first manuscript. One of the mechanisms deployed by tumor cells for immune evasion is to reduce neoantigen presentation through dysregulation of the antigen presentation machinery5. I have performed Machine Learning analysis of single-cell and spatially resolved data from high-plex immunofluorescent images of tumor samples from 265 patients obtained from the Netherlands Cancer Institute. My findings demonstrate distinct dysregulation patterns employed by subtypes of HGSC (1A), leading to varying clinical outcomes (1B). I discovered that MHC-II expression was associated with a better prognosis, leading to increased lymphocyte-cancer cell interactions (1C), supported by whole-slide validation and TCGA RNA-seq data. In addition, differential expression analysis between the groups with low and high MHC+ cancer cells revealed nine genes involved with MHCII antigen presentation, immune signalling and immune-cell maturation and metabolism. These signature genes then were checked in the clinical trial TOPACIO, revealing their association with improved immunotherapy response. I am the 2nd author in the manuscript describing these findings, which has been submitted for publication. 
In the next project, we used tissue Cyclic Immunofluorescence (t-CycIF)6 and single-cell RNA sequencing analyses to investigate the TME in 44 HGSC patients. I have observed a spatial enrichment of proliferating EMT tumor cells in the TME (2A), which is associated with decreased immune-cell interaction (2B) and MCH-I downregulation (2C).

Rationale. We hypothesize that HGSCs with different genotypes will have distinct spatial immunological vulnerabilities, that can be therapeutically exploited to develop more effective immunotherapeutic strategies for patients with HGSC. Our goal is to employ machine learning analysis and multi-omics, single cell, and spatial profiling to discover how the tumor cells escape the immune system by dysregulating MHC-dependent antigen presentation in HGSC subtypes, revealing potential prognostic markers and therapeutic targets for precision oncology.
Objectives of the research and scientific impact of research results 
Obj.1: Identify MHC patterns in HGSC subtypes and their correlation with treatment response and patient survival. Using t-CycIF with 33 markers, we profiled single-cell spatial landscapes of HGSC in 265 samples. By analyzing cellular communities and interactions, we revealed distinct MHC dysregulation patterns across HGSC subtypes, shedding light on immune cell interactions and clinical outcomes. This study prepared as a manuscript, in which I contributed as a second author, underscored the pivotal role of antigen presentation in immune evasion mechanisms and enhanced our understanding of tumor genomics and patient prognosis. 
Obj.2: Elucidate molecular mechanisms of MHC dysregulation's impact on immune responses and tumor progression in HGSC. Utilizing t-CycIF and Nanostring GeoMx spatial transcriptomics, we'll profile gene and protein expression in tumor, immune, and stromal cells.

idate molecular mechanisms of MHC dysregulation's impact on immune responses and tumor progression in HGSC. Utilizing t-CycIF and Nanostring GeoMx spatial transcriptomics, we'll profile gene and protein expression in tumor, immune, and stromal cells. We aim to understand molecular programs driving MHC-dependent immune escape across HGSC genotypes, correlating MHC dysregulation with clinical outcomes. Furthermore, we hypothesize distinct roles and spatial features of lymphocytes, including CD4+ T cells, across HGSC subtypes, which may offer insights into prognostic and therapeutic targets.
These studies establish a foundation for elucidating immune evasion mechanisms in HGSC, informing personalized treatment approaches and guiding drug development initiatives. Furthermore, they contribute to broader public health goals by informing strategies for cancer prevention, early detection, and treatment across various malignancies.
Research methods and material 
Research methods and materials to be used and its significance for the research project (max. 3000 characters with spaces) 
Patient samples. 
Obj.1.The cohort from the Netherlands Cancer Institute comprises 265 HGSC patients. Clinical data from the Netherlands Cancer Registry, histopathological data, and Formalin-fixed, paraffin-embedded (FFPE) tissue blocks were obtained from the nationwide Dutch Pathology Registry. All patients underwent tumor profiling to assess genomic alterations.
Obj.2.The Oncosys-Ova study, initiated in October 2019, has enrolled up to 400 patients with HGSC as of now. Both fresh and FFPE tumor samples, along with associated clinical and sequencing data, were collected.
t-CycIF highly-multiplexed single-cell imaging (Obj.1&2).

a study, initiated in October 2019, has enrolled up to 400 patients with HGSC as of now. Both fresh and FFPE tumor samples, along with associated clinical and sequencing data, were collected.
t-CycIF highly-multiplexed single-cell imaging (Obj.1&2).
Utilizing t-CycIF imaging of FFPE samples enables multiplexed single-cell spatial analysis at the protein level, allowing simultaneous detection of up to 60 targets via cyclic staining of FFPE samples with commercially available and validated antibodies. This methodology and pipelines are implemented at the Färkkilä Lab, University of Helsinki, incorporating computational algorithms for image processing, visualization, and spatial analysis. 
Machine learning analysis of patient tumor samples (Obj.1).
An In-house Random Forest (RF)-based pipeline for biological features extraction was created during my master’s program. Leveraging this pipeline, we analyzed single-cell and spatially resolved data from tumor samples to discern the relationship between TME characteristics, molecular profiles, and patient outcomes. This approach helped to identify patterns and associations between MHC dysregulation and immune evasion mechanisms in diverse HGSC subtypes, informing hypotheses regarding the prognostic significance of MHC expression.
Spatial transcriptomics (Obj.2).
We will perform Nanostring GeoMX spatial transcriptomics on regions of interest from FFPE samples guided by t-CycIF. Processed and deconvoluted data will be analyzed using a lab-developed pipeline to provide insights into the molecular programs underlying MHC-dysregulated tumor cells and immune cell interactions, leading to immune evasion.
Integration and analysis of tCycIF and GeoMx (Obj.2).

ta will be analyzed using a lab-developed pipeline to provide insights into the molecular programs underlying MHC-dysregulated tumor cells and immune cell interactions, leading to immune evasion.
Integration and analysis of tCycIF and GeoMx (Obj.2).
We will employ a unique pipeline integrating t-CycIF single-cell spatial information with spatial transcriptomics developed collaboratively at the lab. This approach provides a complete overlay of the two technologies, allowing the characterization of the spatial molecular landscape of HGSC tumors. It will facilitate the identification of key biological pathways and potential therapeutic targets associated with MHC dysregulation and immune evasion mechanisms.

Title of the doctoral thesis (preliminary): Spatial multi-omics profiling of immune recognition in

Background and aims of the research project 
Summary of the research plan 
High-grade serous ovarian cancer (HGSC) poses significant clinical challenges due to its aggressive 
nature and poor prognosis. Genetic alterations, particularly in DNA-repair homologous recombination, 
contribute to distinct molecular phenotypes in HGSC. While major histocompatibility complex (MHC) 
has been implicated in anti-tumor immunity, its specific role in immune evasion mechanisms in HGSC 
subtypes remains underexplored. 
Our research focuses on elucidating the role of MHC dysregulation in HGSC. Through a 
multidisciplinary approach involving machine learning analysis of patient tumor samples, spatial 
characterization of the tumor microenvironment, and cell-cell interaction studies, we aim to uncover 
molecular programs underlying MHC-dependent immune evasion and tumor progression in HGSC. By 
identifying potential prognostic markers and therapeutic targets, our study aims to lay the 
groundwork for personalized treatment approaches and improved management of HGSC patients. 
Rationale for the research project 
Tumorigenesis and cancer progression depend on malignant cells' ability to escape the recognition 
and attack by the host immune system1. HGSC is characterized by Homologous Recombination DNA 
repair deficiency (HRD) in 50% of the tumors2,3. Emerging evidence suggests that the HGCSs with HRD 
harbor distinct immune escape mechanisms4.

Preliminary data & supporting results from first manuscript. One of the mechanisms deployed by 
tumor cells for immune evasion is to reduce neoantigen presentation through dysregulation of the 
antigen presentation machinery5. I have performed Machine Learning analysis of single-cell and 
spatially resolved data from high-plex immunofluorescent images of tumor samples from 265 patients 
obtained from the Netherlands Cancer Institute. My findings demonstrate distinct dysregulation 
patterns employed by subtypes of HGSC (1A), leading to varying clinical outcomes (1B). I discovered 
that MHC-II expression was associated with a better prognosis, leading to increased lymphocyte -
cancer cell interactions (1C). I am the 2nd author in the manuscript describing these findings, which 
will be published by the end of spring 2024.

In the next project, we used tissue Cyclic Immunofluorescence (t-CycIF)6 and single-cell RNA 
sequencing analyses to investigate the TME in 44 HGSC patients. I have observed a spatial enrichment 
of proliferating EMT tumor cells in the TME (2A), which is associated with decreased immune-cell 
interaction (2B) and MCH-I downregulation (2C).

Briefly describe your studies, work, and other experiences so far. Additionally, provide

your dissertation within three years. 
 
My interdisciplinary background in biomedicine and information technology, coupled with my 
research experiences, positions me to contribute meaningfully to the field of Precision Cancer 
Medicine and successfully complete my dissertation within the allotted timeframe. 
Throughout my academic journey, I have cultivated a diverse skill set and a deep passion for exploring 
the intersection of biology and technology. I completed my undergraduate degree in Science and 
Technology at the University of Tartu, where I gained foundational knowledge in both fields. 
Subsequently, I pursued a master's program in Genetics and Molecular Biosciences at the University 
of Helsinki, delving deeper into the intricacies of cellular biology, molecular genetics, and immunology. 
During my undergraduate degree, I had the privilege of collaborating with Professor Osamu Shimmi's 
research group on various projects, ranging from CRISPR/Cas9 gene editing to the analysis of 
immunofluorescently stained tissues. Participation in these projects not only gave me a chance to 
receive experience in genetic experiments, their analysis, and building a hypothesis based on this, but 
also boosted the self-management skills crucial for future research. I have received feedback on the

ese projects not only gave me a chance to 
receive experience in genetic experiments, their analysis, and building a hypothesis based on this, but 
also boosted the self-management skills crucial for future research. I have received feedback on the 
fast-learning pace, strong commitment to the research, and its independent troubleshooting. 
Subsequently, I pursued a master's program in Genetics and Molecular Biosciences, where I deepened 
my understanding of cancer biology, immune response in cancer, and informatics. In addition, I had a 
chance to perform Erasmus+ mobility at Sorbonne University. I explored systems immunology and 
antitumoral immunity, gaining invaluable knowledge about tools to tackle immunological questions 
and analyze such data. Moreover, I received a highly competitive HiLIFE research scholarship to 
complete a four-month internship analyzing cellular interactions in the ovarian cancer 
microenvironment using machine learning. In this role, I was tasked with employing machine learning 
techniques to extract biological features from single-cell and spatially resolved data from High-grade 
Serous cancer (HGSC) samples. Through proactive problem-solving and collaborative effort, I 
successfully developed a pipeline that utilizes Random Forest's feature selection capabilities to 
identify relevant features of the tumor microenvironment associated with the clinicomolecular 
characteristics of HGSC. Furthermore, we anticipate publishing our research findings by the end of 
spring 2024, where my contribution will be acknowledged with second authorship. This experience

RESEARCH PLAN 
Applicant´s name: María, Hincapié Otero
Start date of study right: 01.05.2024
Title of the doctoral thesis (preliminary): Personalized Treatment Strategies for Ovarian Cancer using Heterotypic Patient-Derived 3D Cultures 
Background and aims of the research project
Summary of the research plan
High-grade serous ovarian cancer (HGSC) poses significant challenges due to its aggressive nature and poor prognosis. Conventional treatments often lack long-term efficacy, resulting in high relapse rates. Recent studies advocate for a promising approach to enhance outcomes: targeting different cell types in the tumor microenvironment (TME), rather than solely focusing on proliferative cancer cells. For example, HGSC tumors with DNA repair deficiency display increased immune cell infiltration, indicating susceptibility to immunotherapies. However, only half of the patients carry these deficiencies, underscoring the need for broader TME exploration. This project aims to develop a novel patient-derived heterotypic 3D culture platform for comprehensive HGSC TME study. By integrating fibroblasts into an existing system, we aim to accurately mimic TME complexity, enabling high-throughput drug screening to identify novel effective drug combinations for clinical translation. 
Rationale for the research project 
Despite the initial treatment response, 70-80% of HGSC patients relapse within three years, underscoring the urgent need for novel effective therapies. Medical decisions regarding treatment options for specific patients are overly complex. In this sense, various ex-vivo models have been developed as potential drug testing platforms to guide therapeutic selection in clinical settings.

tive therapies. Medical decisions regarding treatment options for specific patients are overly complex. In this sense, various ex-vivo models have been developed as potential drug testing platforms to guide therapeutic selection in clinical settings. However, most of these models are supported by animal-derived matrices, limiting their biological relevance and, therefore, the translational potential of associated findings. Recently, the Färkkilä laboratory optimized a new method to establish immune-competent patient-derived cultures (iPDCs) using a human omentum-derived matrix, a system that faithfully recapitulates many of the characteristics of the HGSC TME (Fig 1). Notably, iPDCs supports tumor-derived immune cell growth1 (Fig. 1A,C), facilitating immuno-oncology drug testing.
Although our iPDCs surpasses many other models (Fig 1B), it still lacks important components of the HGSC TME. For this, my project aims to introduce cancer-associated fibroblasts (CAFs) into our iPDCs. This enhanced platform will better represent the complexity of the TME, allowing for a more biologically relevant exploration of potential treatment regimes. The theoretical premise of this project lies on the recognition of the TME as a key determinant of HGSC therapy response2,3: by targeting multiple components of the TME, including fibroblasts, we aim to identify novel therapeutics with the potential to improve patient outcomes and overcome treatment resistance in the future. Methodologically, this project builds upon a well-established 3D culture and incorporates optimization strategies to integrate CAFs effectively. This approach will enable us to study TME interactions in a controlled environment.
Objectives of the research and scientific impact of research results

-established 3D culture and incorporates optimization strategies to integrate CAFs effectively. This approach will enable us to study TME interactions in a controlled environment.
Objectives of the research and scientific impact of research results 
We hypothesize that culturing patient-derived CAFs, immune and cancer cells together as a 3D tri-culture will better mimic the complexity of the in vivo HGSC TME, enabling accurate identification of novel effective drug combinations for HGSC (Fig. 2). Two specific aims arise from this hypothesis:
Aim 1: Establish a novel patient-derived 3D tri-culture including CAFs, immune and cancer cells embedded on a human omentum-derived matrix.
Optimize CAFs isolation and purification protocols.
Optimize culturing conditions to sustain to sustain the growth of all cell types.
Validate the system through stromal and tumor profiling of matched tumors and cultures.
Aim 2: Employ the established patient-derived 3D tri-cultures as a functional high-throughput platform to identify novel effective drug combinations for HGSC.
Conduct high-throughput imaging-based screening of a pre-selected drug panel in 30 cultures.
Identify the top 5 most promising drug combinations based on cancer cell cytotoxic responses.
Characterize functional responses from immune cells and CAFs by performing flow cytometry analysis on the established cultures treated with the top 5 drug combinations.
Beyond its scientific impact, this project aims to extend its reach by potentially translating identified drug combinations into future clinical trials. Moreover, it represents a significant advancement in the ongoing initiative to reduce animal model usage, demonstrating substantial impact across sectors.
Research methods and material

Research methods and material 
Research methods and materials to be used and its significance for the research project 
Patient samples: The Färkkilä laboratory collects freshly dissected tissue from HGSC patients in the prospective ONCOSYS-OVA study weekly, ensuring an abundant supply of the required material for this project (over 350 samples to date). Additionally, we collect and store other materials and associated data, including paraffin-embedded tissue blocks, bulk RNA-seq, whole exome sequencing, BRCA1/2 mutational profiling, genomic scarring analysis, and comprehensive surgical, pathological, and clinical data, providing extensive resources for future utilization.
Establishment of a novel ex-vivo model of the HGSC TME: I will enhance the iPDCs established in the Färkkilä laboratory1 (Fig 2A), by incorporating CAFs (Fig. 2B). Initially, I will isolate and purify CAFs from fresh tumor tissues, employing various digestion methods and magnetic-activated cell sorting. Promising results from preliminary experiments (Fig. 3) will facilitate the progression to the next step of the project: optimization of different culturing parameters. Comparisons between original tumors and established cultures will also be conducted. With prior iPDCs experience, including drug testing experiments resulting in co-authorship of a paper currently under review in Cancer Cell4, and the abundant in-house resources and expertise at the Färkkilä laboratory, this project is set for success.

prior iPDCs experience, including drug testing experiments resulting in co-authorship of a paper currently under review in Cancer Cell4, and the abundant in-house resources and expertise at the Färkkilä laboratory, this project is set for success. 
High-throughput drug testing: Upon establishing the enhanced system, I will use it as a high-throughput drug testing platform, similar to my previous work with iPDCs in the Färkkilä laboratory (Fig 2C). Stromal and Immune cell abundance and functional states in response to treatments will be assessed at a single-cell level using an already optimized 14-plexed flow cytometry panel on the BD LSRFortessaTM Cell Analyzer at the Hartman Institute. Also, the Opera Phenix Plus High Content Screening System from FIMM will enable single-cell image-based analysis of cancer cells cytotoxic responses. This comprehensive approach will yield valuable insights into the therapeutic potential of novel drug combinations.
Data analysis and visualization: The Färkkilä Laboratory has a proven track record in developing tools for analyzing multiparameter flow cytometry and high-content imaging data. In-house developed tools such as CYTO5 and TRIBUS6 are cell type calling algorithms that will streamline phenotyping and extraction of complex biological features from my datasets (Fig 2C). Moreover, a range of in-house R scripts for generating visual representations, including heatmaps, dot plots, and bar plots, are readily available. Together with the expertise of bioinformatics experts in the laboratory, these resources greatly enhance the feasibility of this project.

Preliminary plan on the collection, usage, and storage of the research material 
This project will leverage tissue samples and clinical data collected since 2019 from the ongoing ONCOSYS-Ova prospective study. I will strictly adhere to the Färkkilä laboratory protocols for sample and clinical data management. These protocols provide detailed instructions on processing all received samples and safeguarding clinical information. To store newly generated data, I will take advantage of different available platforms such as the University of Helsinki Datacloud and L-Drive. The Färkkilä laboratory has a well-defined IT infrastructure with clear guidelines for categorizing and storing files on the mentioned platforms based on their nature, sensitivity, and size, ensuring organized and efficient data management. Furthermore, upon publication of results, all raw data files will be accessible to other researchers via public databases, promoting transparency and collaboration. 
Ethical issues 
The prospective clinical study ONCOSYS-Ova has obtained ethical approval from Helsinki University Hospital, the Women’s Hospital, and the Helsinki Biobank. Each participating patient has been comprehensively briefed on all project aspects at recruitment and has willingly given informed consent for sample acquisition, clinical data, and subsequent analysis. Furthermore, all the experimenters involved in the project are required to sign a confidentiality agreement to ensure that all the data is handled anonymously, in agreement with the ethical principles of the Helsinki University Hospital and the EU.

Furthermore, all the experimenters involved in the project are required to sign a confidentiality agreement to ensure that all the data is handled anonymously, in agreement with the ethical principles of the Helsinki University Hospital and the EU. These protocols span from the moment of sample collection to the publication of research findings in scientific journals, ensuring adherence to ethical standards throughout the entire research process.

Title of the doctoral thesis (preliminary): Personalized Treatment Strategies for Ovarian Cancer using Heterotypic Patient-Derived 3D Cultures 
Background and aims of the research project
Summary of the research plan
High-grade serous ovarian cancer (HGSC) poses significant challenges due to its aggressive nature and poor prognosis. Conventional treatments often lack long-term efficacy, resulting in high relapse rates. Recent studies advocate for a promising approach to enhance outcomes: targeting different cell types in the tumor microenvironment (TME), rather than solely focusing on proliferative cancer cells. For example, HGSC tumors with DNA repair deficiency display increased immune cell infiltration, indicating susceptibility to immunotherapies. However, only half of the patients carry these deficiencies, underscoring the need for broader TME exploration. This project aims to develop a novel patient-derived heterotypic 3D culture platform for comprehensive HGSC TME study. By integrating fibroblasts into an existing system, we aim to accurately mimic TME complexity, enabling high-throughput drug screening to identify novel effective drug combinations for clinical translation. 
Rationale for the research project 
Despite the initial treatment response, 70-80% of HGSC patients relapse within three years, underscoring the urgent need for novel effective therapies. Medical decisions regarding treatment options for specific patients are overly complex. In this sense, various ex-vivo models have been developed as potential drug testing platforms to guide therapeutic selection in clinical settings.

tive therapies. Medical decisions regarding treatment options for specific patients are overly complex. In this sense, various ex-vivo models have been developed as potential drug testing platforms to guide therapeutic selection in clinical settings. However, most of these models are supported by animal-derived matrices, limiting their biological relevance and, therefore, the translational potential of associated findings. Recently, the Färkkilä laboratory optimized a new method to establish immune-competent patient-derived cultures (iPDCs) using a human omentum-derived matrix, a system that faithfully recapitulates many of the characteristics of the HGSC TME (Fig 1). Notably, iPDCs supports tumor-derived immune cell growth1 (Fig. 1A,C), facilitating immuno-oncology drug testing.
Although our iPDCs surpasses many other models (Fig 1B), it still lacks important components of the HGSC TME. For this, my project aims to introduce cancer-associated fibroblasts (CAFs) into our iPDCs. This enhanced platform will better represent the complexity of the TME, allowing for a more biologically relevant exploration of potential treatment regimes. The theoretical premise of this project lies on the recognition of the TME as a key determinant of HGSC therapy response2,3: by targeting multiple components of the TME, including fibroblasts, we aim to identify novel therapeutics with the potential to improve patient outcomes and overcome treatment resistance in the future. Methodologically, this project builds upon a well-established 3D culture and incorporates optimization strategies to integrate CAFs effectively. This approach will enable us to study TME interactions in a controlled environment.
Objectives of the research and scientific impact of research results

-established 3D culture and incorporates optimization strategies to integrate CAFs effectively. This approach will enable us to study TME interactions in a controlled environment.
Objectives of the research and scientific impact of research results 
We hypothesize that culturing patient-derived CAFs, immune and cancer cells together as a 3D tri-culture will better mimic the complexity of the in vivo HGSC TME, enabling accurate identification of novel effective drug combinations for HGSC (Fig. 2). Two specific aims arise from this hypothesis:
Aim 1: Establish a novel patient-derived 3D tri-culture including CAFs, immune and cancer cells embedded on a human omentum-derived matrix.
Optimize CAFs isolation and purification protocols.
Optimize culturing conditions to sustain the growth of all cell types.
Validate the system through stromal and tumor profiling of matched tumors and cultures.
Aim 2: Employ the established patient-derived 3D tri-cultures as a functional high-throughput platform to identify novel effective drug combinations for HGSC.
Conduct high-throughput imaging-based screening of a pre-selected drug panel in 30 cultures.
Identify the top 5 most promising drug combinations based on cancer cell cytotoxic responses.
Characterize functional responses from immune cells and CAFs by performing flow cytometry analysis on the established cultures treated with the top 5 drug combinations.
Beyond its scientific impact, this project aims to extend its reach by potentially translating identified drug combinations into future clinical trials. Moreover, it represents a significant advancement in the ongoing initiative to reduce animal model usage, demonstrating substantial impact across sectors.

Mitigation plan: As shown in Table 2, a thorough risk assessment has been conducted, identifying potential risks and mitigation strategies to ensure project safety and successful completion.
Funding plan: All infrastructure, materials, and reagents have been secured by the Färkkilä Laboratory funding. RESEARCH PLAN 
Applicant´s name: María, Hincapié Otero
Start date of study right: 01.05.2024
Title of the doctoral thesis (preliminary): Personalized Treatment Strategies for Ovarian Cancer using Heterotypic Patient-Derived 3D Cultures 
Background and aims of the research project
Summary of the research plan
High-grade serous ovarian cancer (HGSOC) poses significant challenges due to its aggressive nature and poor prognosis. Conventional treatments often lack long-term efficacy, resulting in high relapse rates. Recent studies advocate for a promising approach to enhance outcomes: targeting different cell types in the tumor microenvironment (TME), rather than solely focusing on proliferative cancer cells. For example, HGSOC tumors with DNA repair deficiency display increased immune cell infiltration, indicating susceptibility to immunotherapies. However, only half of the patients carry these deficiencies, underscoring the need for broader TME exploration. This project aims to develop a novel patient-derived heterotypic 3D culture platform for comprehensive HGSOC TME study. By integrating fibroblasts into an existing system, we aim to accurately mimic TME complexity, enabling high-throughput drug screening to identify novel combinations and predictive biomarkers for clinical translation. 
Rationale for the research project 
Despite the initial treatment response, 70-80% of HGSOC patients relapse within three years, underscoring the urgent need for novel effective therapies. Medical decisions regarding treatment options for specific patients are overly complex. In this sense, various ex-vivo models have been developed as potential drug testing platforms to guide therapeutic selection in clinical settings.

tive therapies. Medical decisions regarding treatment options for specific patients are overly complex. In this sense, various ex-vivo models have been developed as potential drug testing platforms to guide therapeutic selection in clinical settings. However, most of these models are supported by animal-derived matrices, limiting their biological relevance and, therefore, the translational potential of associated findings. Recently, the Färkkilä laboratory optimized a new method to establish immune-competent patient-derived cultures (iPDCs) using a human omentum-derived matrix, a novel system that faithfully recapitulates many of the characteristics of the HGSOC TME (Fig 1A). Remarkably, this system supports tumor-derived immune cell growth (Fig. 1C), facilitating immuno-oncology drug testing.
Although our iPDCs surpasses many other models (Fig 1B), it still lacks important components of the HGSOC TME. For this, my project aims to introduce cancer-associated fibroblasts (CAFs) into our iPDCs. This innovative platform will better represent the complexity of the TME, allowing for a more biologically relevant exploration of potential drug targets and treatment modalities. The theoretical premise of this project lies on the recognition of the TME as a key determinant of HGSOC therapy response: by targeting multiple components of the TME, including fibroblasts, we aim to identify novel therapeutics with the potential to improve patient outcomes and overcome treatment resistance in the future. Methodologically, this project builds upon a well-established 3D culture and incorporates optimization strategies to integrate CAFs effectively.

rapeutics with the potential to improve patient outcomes and overcome treatment resistance in the future. Methodologically, this project builds upon a well-established 3D culture and incorporates optimization strategies to integrate CAFs effectively. This approach will enable us to study TME interactions in a controlled environment, facilitating the identification of promising drug candidates and predictive biomarkers for clinical translation.
Objectives of the research and scientific impact of research results 
This project primarily aims to develop an innovative patient-derived 3D tri-culture for studying treatment responses in HGSOC with potential to improve therapeutic strategies (Fig. 2). This goal comprises three specific aims:
Aim 1: Optimize culture conditions to establish a patient-derived 3D tri-culture including fibroblasts, immune and cancer cells.
Hypothesis: Culturing fibroblasts, immune and cancer cells together in a heterotypic 3D tri-culture will better mimic HGSOC TME.
Aim 2: Utilize the established patient-derived 3D tri-cultures as a high-throughput platform to screen and identify novel effective immuno-oncology drug combinations against HGSOC.
Hypothesis: Patient-derived 3D tri-cultures enable the identification of novel effective immuno-oncology drugs and/or drug combinations against HGSOC.
Aim 3: Identify novel predictive biomarkers associated with both drug sensitivity and resistance, enhancing our understanding of treatment response variability in HGSOC patients.
Hypothesis: A comprehensive analysis of treatment response variability in HGSOC patients will suggest novel predictive biomarkers associated with drug sensitivity and resistance.

our understanding of treatment response variability in HGSOC patients.
Hypothesis: A comprehensive analysis of treatment response variability in HGSOC patients will suggest novel predictive biomarkers associated with drug sensitivity and resistance.
By achieving these objectives, this project will impact not only the scientific community but also beyond academia. For instance, other researchers could leverage the proposed 3D tri-culture for mechanistic studies, while patients stand to benefit from the newly identified drug combinations and biomarkers. 
Research methods and material 
Research methods and materials to be used and its significance for the research project 
Patient samples: The Färkkilä laboratory receives freshly dissected tissue samples from different HGSOC patients enrolled in the prospective ONCOSYS-Ova study weekly. With over 350 samples collected to date, there is an abundant supply of material ensuring sufficient patient-derived samples for my project's development.
Establishment of a novel ex-vivo model of the HGSOC TME: Leveraging the iPDCs recently developed 
in the Färkkilä laboratory, I will create an enhanced model incorporating CAFs (Fig. 2A). I will begin by isolating and purifying CAFs from fresh tumor tissues, utilizing various digestion methods and magnetic-activated cell sorting. Preliminary experiments have yielded promising results (Fig. 3), facilitating the progression to the next step of the project: optimization of different culturing parameters.

s, utilizing various digestion methods and magnetic-activated cell sorting. Preliminary experiments have yielded promising results (Fig. 3), facilitating the progression to the next step of the project: optimization of different culturing parameters. My prior experience working with iPDCs, demonstrated through participation in drug testing experiments leading to co-authorship of a research paper currently under review in Cancer Cell, along with the extensive in-house resources and human capital available within the Färkkilä laboratory, position this project for success.
High-throughput drug testing: Upon establishing the enhanced system, I will employ it as a high-throughput drug testing platform, similar to how I have been using the iPDCs in the Färkkilä laboratory (Fig 2B). Immune cell abundance and functional states in response to different treatments will be assessed using an optimized 14-plexed flow cytometry panel on the BD LSRFortessaTM Cell Analyzer located at the Hartman Institute. Additionally, the Opera Phenix Plus High Content Screening System from FIMM will facilitate image-based analysis of cytotoxic responses from cancer cells. This comprehensive approach will provide valuable insights into the therapeutic potential of novel drug combinations.
Biomarker discovery: Tissue-based cyclic immunofluorescence is a technique that allows the simultaneous detection of up to 100 different antigens using cyclic staining of FFPE samples and commercially available antibodies. By combining insights from drug responses in the enhanced 3D cultures and t-CycIF, I aim to discover predictive biomarkers for individualized therapeutic responses (Fig 2C).

using cyclic staining of FFPE samples and commercially available antibodies. By combining insights from drug responses in the enhanced 3D cultures and t-CycIF, I aim to discover predictive biomarkers for individualized therapeutic responses (Fig 2C). To achieve this, I will use the RARECYTE CyteFinder II instrument in the Färkkilä Laboratory to generate highly-multiplexed images from FFPE blocks of ONCOSYS-OVA patients and fixed 3D cultures. Supported by the sophisticated t-CycIF pipelines established in the Färkkilä laboratory and our international collaborations with the developers of the technique at Harvard, I am well-equipped to successfully complete this part of my project. 
Data analysis: The Färkkilä Laboratory has a proven track record of developing bioinformatic tools for analyzing both multiparameter flow cytometry and high-content imaging data. Established tools include CYTO and TRIBUS, which enable comprehensive analysis of flow cytometry-generated and high-content imaging data. This will facilitate the extraction of complex biological features from my datasets.

However, existing HGSC 3D models often lack essential tumor microenvironment (TME) components and rely on animal-derived matrices, limiting their precision medicine potential. To overcome this clinical challenge, my project aims to establish an innovative cell culture platform that will better recapitulate the HGSC TME. I will integrate cancer-associated fibroblasts (CAFs) into an immune-competent system embedded in a human matrix, creating an enhanced platform for rapid identification of promising drug combinations through high-throughput screenings. By assessing cytotoxicity on cancer cells and stromal activation states via high-content imaging and flow cytometry, my research will contribute to the selection of viable candidates for future clinical trials.

Objectives of the research and scientific impact of research results (max. 1500 characters with spaces)
We hypothesize that culturing patient-derived CAFs, immune and cancer cells together as a 3D tri-culture will better mimic the complexity of the in vivo HGSC TME, enabling accurate identification of novel effective drug combinations for HGSC (Fig. 2). Two specific aims arise from this hypothesis:
Aim 1: Establish a novel patient-derived 3D tri-culture including CAFs, immune and cancer cells embedded on a human omentum-derived matrix.
Optimize CAFs isolation and purification protocols.
Optimize culturing conditions to sustain the growth of all cell types.
Validate the system through stromal and tumor profiling of matched tumors and cultures.
Aim 2: Employ the established patient-derived 3D tri-cultures as a functional high-throughput platform to identify novel effective drug combinations for HGSC.
Conduct high-throughput imaging-based screening of a pre-selected drug panel in 30 cultures.
Identify the top 5 most promising drug combinations based on cancer cell cytotoxic responses.
Characterize functional responses from immune cells and CAFs by performing flow cytometry analysis on the established cultures treated with the top 5 drug combinations.
Beyond its scientific impact, this project aims to extend its reach by translating identified drug combinations into future clinical trials. Moreover, it represents a significant advancement in the ongoing initiative to reduce animal experiments, demonstrating substantial impact across sectors.

Sample collection: The Färkkilä laboratory collects freshly dissected tissue from HGSC patients in the prospective ONCOSYS-Ova study weekly, ensuring abundant supply of the required material for this project (over 350 samples to date). Additionally, we collect and store other materials and associated data, including paraffin-embedded tissue blocks, bulk RNA-seq, whole exome sequencing, BRCA1/2 mutational profiling, genomic scarring analysis, and detailed surgical, pathological, and clinical data, providing extensive resources for future utilization.
Establishment of a novel ex-vivo model of the HGSC TME: I will enhance the iPDCs established in the Färkkilä laboratory1 (Fig 2A), by incorporating CAFs (Fig. 2B). Promising results from preliminary experiments (Fig. 3) facilitate the progression to the next step of the project: optimization of different culturing parameters. Comparisons between original tumors and established cultures will also be conducted. With my extensive prior iPDCs experience, including drug testing experiments resulting in co-authorship of a paper currently under review in Cancer Cell4, and the abundant in-house resources and expertise at the Färkkilä laboratory, this project is set for success.

RESEARCH PLAN 
Applicant´s name: Pablo, Siliceo Portugal

Title of the doctoral thesis (preliminary): Spatiotemporal analysis of tumors deficient in homologous recombination
Background and aims of the research project
Summary of the research plan (max. 1000 characters)
The study of the spatial architecture of the tumor microenvironment (TME) has paved the way for exciting new immunotherapeutic interventions. Homologous recombination deficient (HRD) tumors are known to have distinctive tumor-immune interactions that help stratify patients into more effective treatment regimes2. Nonetheless, the importance of the 3D architecture of tumors during tumor initiation, development, and resistance to treatment has yet to be fully explored5. By implementing spatial proteomics (t-CycIF), transcriptomics (GeoMX), and deep computational and mathematical analyses, our project aims to:
Elucidate early regulatory processes that drive tumor development in HRD tumors; explore the chemotherapy-induced spatiotemporal remodeling of the tumor’s 3D cell architecture; and, investigate key spatiotemporal features involved in pathogenesis and clinical outcomes.
Rationale for the research project (max. 2000 characters with spaces)
The evasion of immune surveillance and induction of hypofunctional immunophenotypes by tumor cells represents a major hallmark in cancer development1. New therapies targeting the TME provide alternatives to the majority of first line treatments in HGSC, which are based on DNA damaging agents regardless of the increasing probability of developing chemoresistance3.
Previous research: During my bachelor’s dissertation with Dr. Rodríguez, in collaboration with Dr.

ority of first line treatments in HGSC, which are based on DNA damaging agents regardless of the increasing probability of developing chemoresistance3.
Previous research: During my bachelor’s dissertation with Dr. Rodríguez, in collaboration with Dr. Färkkilä, I performed t-CycIF in HRD tumors from patients with cancer-prone syndrome Fanconi Anemia (with inherited mutations on genes upstream of the HR pathway) and found that these tumors had an increased, but ineffective, immune infiltration (Fig 1A) characterized by a correlation between co-localizing tumor associated macrophages (TAMs) and T cells with immune exhaustion (TIM3+) at the tumor-stroma interface (Fig 1B and 1C). On the other hand, Dr. Färkillä’s group found in HGSC tumors (with acquired mutations on genes downstream of the HR pathway) that infiltrating myeloid cells form intricate networks (myelonets) that foster T cell exhaustion in patients responding to chemotherapy4. Despite these insights, there is still a lack of information about the mechanistic spatiotemporal evolution of the TME, as well as the interconnectivity of mesoscale features like the 3D architecture of the tumors5.
Rationale: The study of early driver mechanisms of immune evasion, as well as the 3D architecture of the TME by means of single-cell spatial (t-CycIF and GeoMX) profiling will allow a more complete understanding of the evolution of HRD tumors, enabling the identification of promising targets and biomarkers for patient treatment. 
Objectives of the research and scientific impact of research results (max. 1500 characters with spaces)

re complete understanding of the evolution of HRD tumors, enabling the identification of promising targets and biomarkers for patient treatment. 
Objectives of the research and scientific impact of research results (max. 1500 characters with spaces)
To achieve a comprehensive study of the spatiotemporal evolution of the TME in HRD tumors, highlighting key processes, interactions and higher order cellular arrangements whose intervention can be clinically translated into novel immunotherapies, we formulated three main aims:
1. Identify early mechanisms of immune evasion that lead to cancer initiation in HRD tumors Hypothesis 1: Based on our findings on immune exhaustion in FA tumors (a de facto carcinogenesis model), we believe that there exist specific cell-cell interactions and neighborhoods related to the onset of immune exhaustion and carcinogenesis of HRD tumors that are yet to be explored.
1.1 Perform t-CycIF of tumor samples from FA patients
1.2 Investigate significant cell-cell interactions and neighborhoods related to tumor initiation 
2. Study the role of the 3D architecture of the tumor-immune-stromal milieu of HRD tumors with during chemotherapy treatment
Hypothesis 2: 2D interconnected networks of myeloid cells (myelonets) in HRD tumors found in the Oncosys-Ova project are indicative of higher order 3D cellular networks that play a pivotal role in the TME during standard-of-care chemotherapy.
2.1 Perform t-CycIF (Serial sections) and GeoMX (single sections) of HRD tumors
 2.2 Reconstruct computationally the 3D architecture of the tumors TME
2.3 Investigate higher order cell organizations related with functional interactions and their role in immune surveillance and chemoresistance

Research methods and material 
Research methods and materials to be used and its significance for the research project (max. 3000 characters with spaces) 
Patient samples: 
Tumor samples from HGSC patients that underwent chemotherapy are collected through the Oncosys-OVA project (>400 samples). Sections from >20 archived FFPE SCCs from FA patients were donated by the Department of Cytopathology at the University of Düsseldorf, Germany.
H&E staining and annotation: All samples are H&E stained and regions of interest (ROIs) related to tumor classification and tissue heterogeneity are annotated by a pathologist. 
- Progress: 100%. All samples have been collected and processed.
- Contribution: The analysis of FA samples will allow the study of HRD tumor initiation and development due to FA patients’ underlying predisposition to cancer. HGSC samples will represent a platform for the study of the spatial and functional remodeling of the TME during chemotherapy.
t-CycIF highly-multiplexed single-cell imaging: 
Enables the simultaneous detection of >80 different antigens from a single FFPE slide by serial cycles of immunostaining, and scanning with a RareCyte CyteFinder II instrument. This allows the obtention of whole-slide multidimensional images of single or serial (3D) sections of the samples. 
- Progress: 70%. t-CycIF has been done in all FA samples. 30 serial sections from 2 HSGC samples have been processed for 3D reconstruction, we will process 10 more samples.

multidimensional images of single or serial (3D) sections of the samples. 
- Progress: 70%. t-CycIF has been done in all FA samples. 30 serial sections from 2 HSGC samples have been processed for 3D reconstruction, we will process 10 more samples. 
- Contribution: This technology will allow the spatial characterization of the proteomic landscape of both FA and HSGC tumor samples, will help identify key cell types, their connectivity, as well as their involvement in tumorigenesis and the antitumoral response.
GeoMX nanostring spatial transcriptomics: 
Allows the detection of mRNA expression of regions of interest (ROIs) (20-300 cells), profiling transcriptomic signatures and correlating them to spatial features in the tissue. 
- Progress: 50%. GeoMX of HGSC samples has been done in all 2D reconstructions. For 3D reconstructions GeoMX will be done on a single section per sample.
- Contribution: We will gain a deeper understanding of the mechanisms happening within ROIs previously identified by t-CyCIF. This will add a new dimension of data about the regional transcriptional states that can be implemented in the generation of the DMM.
Bioinformatic analysis of spatial features:
The integration and generation of numerous self-made, and Färkkilä’ group, programs enable the interpretation of experimental data through image registration (MCMICRO), visualization (Napari), nuclear segmentation (StarDist), cell-annotation (TRIBUS), 3D reconstruction (Imaris) and data analysis (Seurat and Scimap).
- Progress: 20%. Data analysis of both FA and HSCG 2D data has already been made. Deeper functional variables like RCNs on FA samples and 3D composition in HSGC tumors are still pending.

RESEARCH PLAN Applicant´s name: Wenqing Chen Start date of study right: N/A Title of the doctoral thesis (preliminary): Integrative Single-cell Analysis of the Tumor Microenvironment and Drug Response Signatures in High-Grade Serious Ovarian Cancer Background and aims of the research project Summary of the research plan This research aims to integrate single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) with spatial profiling techniques, including spatial transcriptomics and proteomics to uncover the tumor and immune cell phenotypes associated with resistance in high-grade serious ovarian cancer (HGSC). By analyzing multi-omics data from 24 samples in the discovery set, we will identify key cellular phenotypes and their interaction potential as well as spatial distribution within the tumor microenvironment (TME). Key phenotypes and ligand-receptor interactions will be validated in a larger cohort and functionally tested using patient-derived organoids. This study has the potential to identify novel therapeutic targets and provide a comprehensive understanding of resistance mechanisms in HGSC. Rationale for the research project HGSC is the most lethal gynecological cancer. It is frequently undetected until it has extensively metastasized, largely due to the absence of the effective screening tools and specific symptoms[1]. Moreover, patients experience a high relapse rate after treatment, and their prognosis is significantly impacted by the characteristics of the tumor microenvironment (TME)[2, 3]. Distinct immune cell profiles within the TME are associated with strong immunosuppressive effects[4]. Thus, understanding how the direct TME interactions influence resistant phenotypes in tumor cells is crucial.

he tumor microenvironment (TME)[2, 3]. Distinct immune cell profiles within the TME are associated with strong immunosuppressive effects[4]. Thus, understanding how the direct TME interactions influence resistant phenotypes in tumor cells is crucial. These resistant phenotypes may contribute to tumor’s ability to evade immune detection and resist therapeutic interventions. Additionally, a deeper exploration of these TME interactions could lead to novel approaches to overcome resistance and improve patient outcomes. Previous study has revealed stress-promoted chemoresistance in ovarian cancer through scRNA-seq analysis[2]. While the scRNA-seq has been instrumental in identifying diverse cell types within tumors, it lacks spatial information. Without it, it is difficult to determine how cellular phenotypes are organized and interact within the TME, which is essential for understanding tumor behavior and the discovery of new therapeutic targets. Therefore, integrating scRNA-seq with spatial technologies is essential to improve our understanding of the heterogeneity and complexity of TME in HGSC. This project aims to bridge the gap by combining high-resolution single-cell transcriptomic data with spatial omics techniques, to provide a comprehensive view that includes the molecular signatures and precise positional context of different cell types and states within the tumor. This integrated approach enables more accurate mapping of cellular interactions, validating the interactions predicted by ligand-receptor (L-R) analysis, and the architectural organization of TME. It thus offers deeper insights into the underlying mechanisms that drive tumor progression and therapeutic resistance in ovarian cancer.

lidating the interactions predicted by ligand-receptor (L-R) analysis, and the architectural organization of TME. It thus offers deeper insights into the underlying mechanisms that drive tumor progression and therapeutic resistance in ovarian cancer. Methodologically, this project is innovative in its integration of advanced sequencing and imaging technologies, including scRNA-seq, spatial transcriptomics (GeoMX), and imaging-based spatial proteomics (tCyCIF). Traditionally, these techniques have been

used separately to study different aspects of TME. However, in this project, we will develop a novel integrative approach that allows for a more comprehensive characterization of the TME. We will perform single-cell spatial analysis at the protein level using tCyCIF imaging of FFPE samples, providing a high-resolution view of the tissue architecture of the same samples that have been analysed with scRNAseq. b. Tools and techniques: The Färkkilä Lab has implemented relevant tCyCIF experimental methods and analysis pipelines, including image processing, cell annotation (TRIBUS), and spatial neighborhoods analysis (Scimap[5], UTAG[6]). 4. Spatial Transcriptomics: a. We will perform Nanostring GeoMX spatial transcriptomics on regions of interest (ROIs) of FFPF samples guided by tCyCIF of the samples profiled with scRNAseq and tCycIF. b.

l neighborhoods analysis (Scimap[5], UTAG[6]). 4. Spatial Transcriptomics: a. We will perform Nanostring GeoMX spatial transcriptomics on regions of interest (ROIs) of FFPF samples guided by tCyCIF of the samples profiled with scRNAseq and tCycIF. b. Tools and techniques: GeoMX data will be analyzed by a pipeline developed by Färkkilä Lab to spatially profile RNA expression in tissue sections, focusing on regions identified in scRNA-seq as critical for resistance. 5. Integration of scRNA-seq, GeoMX and tCyCIF: a. Data integration of them will allow us to correlate cellular phenotypes with their spatial context within the tumor. b. Tools and techniques: We will develop a new computational method to integrate scRNA-seq, GeoMX, and tCyCIF data. This method may be developed based on the relationship between genes and proteins: o Use public gene-protein expression databases (such as Human Protein Atlas) to associate gene expression with protein expression. o Use models that predict protein expression based on transcriptome data. Description of the research environment and the research group The Vähärautio lab combines multidisciplinary expertise in molecular biology and computation to dissect therapy resistance of ovarian cancer on single cell level. The lab is part of the iCAN-OVCA subproject, providing access to clinical HGSC specimens and derived models, and of the Systems Oncology Research Program that provides ample opportunities for low-threshold collaborations among cancer research experts. Both the lab’s proven expertise and access to clinical material are an absolute prerequisite for the proposed research and enable the candidate to grow into a multidisciplinary, translational cancer researcher, focusing on bioinformatics. The Färkkilä lab opened in 2019 and belongs to the highly ranked Research Program in Systems Oncology (ONCOSYS) in the Research Programs Unit at the Faculty of Medicine, UH. The Färkkilä Lab currently consists of three postdoctoral researchers, six PhD students, eight master students/trainees, two technicians, and a research nurse. The lab has all the infrastructure and expertise to complete the proposed project. The lab has shown great track record in graduating six master’s students by the spring of 2024, and two PhD students are planned to defend later in 2024.

Applicant´s name: Zhihan, Liang
