Title of the doctoral thesis (preliminary): Immunogenomic and Spatial Profiling of Adaptive Immunity in Ovarian Cancer
Background and aims of the research project
Summary of the research plan (max. 1000 characters)
High-grade serous ovarian cancer (HGSC) brings huge challenges to clinical treatment due to its aggressive nature and poor prognosis. Genetic alterations and chemotherapy interventions affect the spatial tumor microenvironment (TME) and reshape the dynamic adaptive immunity. This study aims to gain insight into the composition and functional states of B lymphocytes in the TME of HGSC patients and evaluate the impact of tumor- and patient-derived factors. Bulk and single-cell RNA sequencing are used to reveal the immune genomic landscape, especially the B-cell receptor (BCR) repertoire. Spatial single-cell analysis with tissue cyclic immunofluorescence (tCycIF) images and spatial transcriptomics profiling tools including GeoMx and pick-seq are integrated to investigate spatial heterogeneity of B-cell clonality in Tertiary lymphoid structures (TLSs). This will lead to the discovery of potential therapeutic targets and contribute to precision medicine for HGSC.
Rationale for the research project (max. 2000 characters with spaces)
HGSC is the most common and lethal subtype of ovarian cancer. Clinical trials of immune checkpoint inhibitors (ICI) targeting PD-1/PD-L1 have shown limited efficacy in HGSC due to unique suppressed adaptive immunity in TME (Chardin & Leary, 2021).

s)
HGSC is the most common and lethal subtype of ovarian cancer. Clinical trials of immune checkpoint inhibitors (ICI) targeting PD-1/PD-L1 have shown limited efficacy in HGSC due to unique suppressed adaptive immunity in TME (Chardin & Leary, 2021). Immunogenomic and spatial single-cell analysis of HGSC revealed the influence mechanism and prognostic prediction role of specific genotype and immune cells’ spatial interactions on ICI treatment (Färkkilä et al., 2020). This underscores the necessity of enrolling more HGSC patients for large-scale profiling of adaptive immunity with distinct genotypes and clinical features.
Compared with T lymphocytes, the role of B lymphocytes in anti-tumor immunity remains unclear, despite their abundance and spatial heterogeneity in TME. Studies reported the improved prognosis from B cell infiltration in TME, and the impact of B cell gene signatures and BCR repertoire features on patient survival (Downs-Canner, et al., 2022), emphasizing the importance of spatial profiling for B cell gene expression and BCR in TLSs.
In our previous research, single-cell and spatial image analysis based on tCycIF images was applied to TLS areas of HGSC (Fig.1A). All these analysis pipelines will also be implemented in my project. Voronoi plot shows typical locations of various cell types with segmented cells, highlighting B cells abundance and interactions with immune cells (Fig.1B). Based on this, a cooperative expert annotated TLSs for areas of Germinal Centers (GC); T - B cell interaction zone; Myeloid - T cell zone; and Tumor (Fig.1C, solid line). Then I located five targets for all areas except GCs and proceeded with microtissue extraction (Fig.1C, dashed line).

This method allows precise cell collection from distinct locations while retaining their spatial info.
I established this Pick-seq workflow in our lab and achieved its reliable and consistent operation. It will serve as a potent tool for the spatial profiling of TLSs in my project.
Objectives of the research and scientific impact of research results (max. 1500 characters with spaces)
Comprehensive immunogenomic and single-cell profiling of adaptive immunity
Investigate the composition and signaling variations of B lymphocytes across patient subsets with diverse genotypes and clinical features.
Conduct BCR profiling to reveal the heterogeneity and mechanisms of BCR repertoire.
Identify treatment-relevant differential gene expression patterns and pathways to find possible prognostic markers or therapeutic targets.
Spatial profiling of adaptive immunity and TLSs
Perform single-cell spatial analysis of TLSs based on tCycIF images to reveal spatial heterogeneity of lymphocytes and analyze dynamic cell-cell interactions.
Conduct spatial transcriptomics analysis with GeoMx to investigate selected micro-regions of interest (mROIs) and discover key genes.
With annotation of TLSs, use pick-seq to precisely capture specific cell subsets with spatial information and analyze B cell clonality.
We hypothesize that distinct genotypes and clinical subgroups will exhibit variations in both composition and spatial organization of adaptive immunity, in which key immune pathways and therapeutic targets can be found.

By integrating large-scale sequencing and multi-omics methods, our results will show immunogenomic and spatial landscape of adaptive immunity in HGSC. This will provide exceptional potential to unveil immune mechanisms that impact therapeutic outcomes, thereby significantly advancing development of precision oncology.
Research methods and material
Research methods and materials to be used and its significance for the research project (max. 3000 characters with spaces) 
Patient samples: Coordinated by my supervisor Anniina Färkkilä, Oncosys-Ova is a large-scale prospective study in the iCAN Flagship Project iCAN-OVCA conducted by Helsinki University Hospital (HUH) and the University of Helsinki (UH). It started in 2019 and has currently enrolled 400 HGSC patients. These patients provided fresh frozen and formalin-fixed paraffin-embedded (FFPE) samples from primary tumor tissue and metastases with corresponding clinical data and pathology results. We have full accessibility to these ready-to-use clinical samples in our lab to perform t-CycIF and multi-omics analysis, including bulk RNAseq, whole exome sequencing(WES), and spatial transcriptomics.
Immunogenomic & single-cell profiling: The tumor mutations have been identified from WES data of 200 HGSC patients in Oncosys-OVA using Illumina NovaSeq and bioinformatics analysis pipeline Mutect2-GATK. The somatic-specific allelic imbalances will be identified according to the copy-number variation (CNV) profiles using ASCAT.

from WES data of 200 HGSC patients in Oncosys-OVA using Illumina NovaSeq and bioinformatics analysis pipeline Mutect2-GATK. The somatic-specific allelic imbalances will be identified according to the copy-number variation (CNV) profiles using ASCAT. Our new developed ovaHRDscar has been used to annotate tumors based on HRD. Bulk RNAseq data will be analyzed using a state-of-the-art deconvolution algorithm from our department (Häkkinen et al., 2021). Fresh frozen samples of 30 HGSC patients after chemotherapy are used for scRNA seq. Seurat is applied for dimensionality reduction, clustering, and cell type assignment of data after preprocessing, and R package speckle and clusterProfiler will be used for differential cell abundance and over-representation analysis. We also use the MultiNicheNet framework to infer changes in cell-cell interactions.
t-CycIF highly multiplexed single-cell imaging: Single-cell spatial analysis is performed by t-CycIF of FFPE samples with 20 antibodies. t-CycIF protocols, scanners, and analysis pipelines have been implemented in Färkkilä Lab (Fig.4). Spatial heterogeneity and cell-cell interactions are analyzed with genomic and clinical data using machine learning.
Spatial transcriptomics: We perform Nanostring GeoMX spatial transcriptomics on mROIs of FFPE samples guided by t-CycIF. Sequencing data will be analyzed by a pipeline developed in our lab to reveal gene expression signatures of adaptive immunity and find potential therapeutic targets.
B-cell receptor profiling: A cutting-edge method TRUST4 will be used for BCR reconstruction from bulk and single-cell RNA-seq data (Song et al., 2021). The whole workflow of Rarecyte pick-seq has been built in our lab to precisely pick different cell areas in TLS and explore spatial BCR features.

All necessary resources and infrastructure are in place to complete the project in our lab, including all reagents, GeoMX & t-CycIF scanner, and computational analysis pipelines.
Preliminary plan on the collection, usage, and storage of the research material (max. 1000 characters with spaces) 
Patient samples have been collected in the iCAN prospective Oncosys-Ova study. Sensitive data like patient IDs and other clinical data are handled with utmost security and confidentiality and stored in a dedicated OVCA database on the HUH server anonymously. Access to and extraction of clinical data are strictly limited to authorized personnel only via the HUH Datalake provided by the Helsinki Biobank. In the published results, each patient is assigned a randomized publication ID to minimize the possibility of patient identification. All researchers are trained in the lab to process samples and data safely and correctly.
The Färkkilä lab uses computing infrastructure and data analysis software provided by UH, and securely stores data in P-drive & L-drive in UH servers. CSC-IT Center for Science Ltd provides cloud storage and computing services. In our publications, codes and anonymized key data will be stored and shared on GitHub and public databases to facilitate research.

RESEARCH PLAN 
Applicant´s name: Ziqi Kang
Start date of study right: 01.01.2024
Title of the doctoral thesis (preliminary): Computational investigations of the single-cell spatial tumor microenvironment in ovarian cancer
Background and aims of the research project
Summary of the research plan (max. 1000 characters)
Multiplexed immunofluorescence (mIF) ensures spatial information and provides insights into tumor microenvironment (TME). However, due to the complexity of tissue architecture, new approaches are needed to integrate multiple levels of TME (de Visser & Joyce, 2023). The research aims to develop the computational toolkit for mIF data and perform comprehensive spatial TME profiling, from (1) a prior-knowledge-based cell-type annotation method to (2) a deep-learning framework for tissue architecture discovery. The toolkit will provide automatic identification for TME elements and integrate with spatial transcriptomics. The research will be based on a large-scale dataset collected from patient-derived high-grade serous ovarian cancer (HGSC) samples from the Oncosys-Ova study. Overall, the research will discover the extensive spatial information within HGSC, identify underlying immune mechanisms, and verify the genomic discoveries in immune escape mechanisms (IEMs).
Rationale for the research project (max. 2000 characters with spaces)
Ovarian cancer (OC) is one of the leading causes of death in gynecological malignancy patients and the most common and lethal subtype of OC is high-grade serous ovarian cancer (HGSC). 
However, due to the high heterogeneity of HGSC, the effect of treatments varies among patients. Thus, understanding the diversity of HGSC is highly needed and tumor microenvironment (TME) is an entry point.

s high-grade serous ovarian cancer (HGSC). 
However, due to the high heterogeneity of HGSC, the effect of treatments varies among patients. Thus, understanding the diversity of HGSC is highly needed and tumor microenvironment (TME) is an entry point. A previous study in our group generated a comprehensive single-cell spatial atlas of TLSs in HGSC using tCyCIF, reporting extensive spatial and phenotypic variations among subtypes of HGSC (Sarkkinen et al., 2023). It was also found that spatial cell-cell interactions could shape the CD8+ T-cell phenotypes and influence the responses to immune checkpoint blockade therapies. 
Various computation tools were designed to study TME. MCMICRO provides an end-to-end processing pipeline to transform multi-channel whole-slide images into single-cell data (Schapiro et al., 2022), achieving accurate cell segmentation and analysis of spatial topologies of cell interaction. With the development of computer vision, techniques such as contrastive learning could be applied to multiplexed immunofluroence (mIF) image patches to extract features in different scales. There is still a lack of automatic ways to study tissue architecture from a higher perspective.
During the project, I aim to identify the specific TME structures in ovarian cancer samples and quantify the extensive molecular landscape and cell-cell interaction information within the diverse tissue compartments. 
Objectives of the research and scientific impact of research results (max. 1500 characters with spaces)
The research aims to profile multiple levels of the tumor microenvironment, target tissue architecture by corresponding computational tools. The research will lead to discoveries in immune escape mechanisms and help with improving ovarian cancer immunotherapies.

ims to profile multiple levels of the tumor microenvironment, target tissue architecture by corresponding computational tools. The research will lead to discoveries in immune escape mechanisms and help with improving ovarian cancer immunotherapies. 
The specific objectives of the project are:
Single-cell resolution tumor microenvironment profiling of high-grade serous ovarian cancer (HGSC) samples based on tCyCIF data. 
Develop new methods for accurate cell type annotation (Tribus) in mIF images.
Perform in-depth profiling of cell properties. Investigate the spatial topographies and cellular communities.
Develop tools for automatic identification of TME features at pixel level and description of specific phenotype profiles within identified spatial structures. 
Develop a deep-learning framework (TMEncoder) to identify tumor microenvironment elements linked to tumor histology, genomic features, and patient clinical outcomes. 
Apply visual explanations based on the output of the framework, automatic Regions of Interest (ROI) selection for downstream GeoMX analysis. 
Association of highly multiplexed spatial phenotype features (tCyCIF) with tumor genomic features (GeoMX). Considering the physical proximity of cellular objects, identify novel microanatomical domains and explore cell-cell interaction.
Research methods and material 
Research methods and materials to be used and its significance for the research project (max. 3000 characters with spaces) 
In-house materials
All materials and infrastructure are in place to complete the Projects. Up to 400 HGSC samples have been collected in the prospective Oncosys-OVA clinical study at Helsinki University Hospital; those samples are also included in the iCAN Flagship Project.

aterials and infrastructure are in place to complete the Projects. Up to 400 HGSC samples have been collected in the prospective Oncosys-OVA clinical study at Helsinki University Hospital; those samples are also included in the iCAN Flagship Project. Both fresh and formalin-fixed paraffin-embedded (FFPE) samples from the primary tumor tissue and/or metastasis have been collected. The research is based on the whole FFPE slide imaging dataset generated using tCycIF - a multiplex fluorescence microscopy method. 
Single-cell phenotyping
A semi-supervised cell-type calling tool Tribus (Kang et al., 2024) will be developed. 
Tribus is based on the self-organized map (SOM) and the input of phenotypic markers as prior knowledge. 
Tribus will be integrated with our in-house image analysis pipeline which is maintained by myself, and applied in other research currently ongoing in our lab.
Automatic pixel-based TME study
I’ll develop a deep-learning-based pipeline “TMEncoder” for whole slide images. 
I'll test the performance of unsupervised discovery of TME features in image patches. Potential methods are (1) contrastive learning technology (Jiménez-Sánchez et al., 2022), (2) latent space embedding generated by variational autoencoder (VAE). 
Similar to cell masks used in multiplexed images, our pipeline will generate niche masks and visualization of marker expression contour plots, thus allowing automatic selections of hotspot regions. Such results could be supplementation for single cell level, but also providing extracellular matrix (ECM) information that was ignored by cell segmentation. I will validate the TME features by mapping them back to pixel maps, and evaluate whether special tissue architectures could be found.
Tissue domains detection combine GeoMX and mIF

Finally, I will focus more on integrating the previously generated results and validate those data-driven findings with genetic and immunogenomic discoveries in my research group. 
Preliminary plan on the collection, usage, and storage of the research material (max. 1000 characters with spaces) 
In the prospective Oncosys-OVA clinical study, a total of 400 high-grade serous ovarian cancer (HGSC) samples have been collected, accompanied by comprehensive clinical information, at Helsinki University Hospital (HUS). This study is under the coordination of Dr. Anniina Färkkilä, who is also the thesis supervisor. The data collected in this research will be stored in the University of Helsinki DataCloud and L-drive, these platforms provide robust environments for safeguarding sensitive datasets. In addition, all clinical and biological data collected in this study will be collected into a customized database (OVCA-Database) at the HUH server. This repository will facilitate the processing and analysis of the collected data in an anonymous manner, thus ensuring privacy. Computational resources for this project are provided by the Färkkilä Lab funding. 
Ethical issues (max. 1000 characters with spaces) 
Informed consent has been provided by all patients included in the Oncosys-OVA clinical study for the collection of their fresh tumor samples and clinical data. The study has been approved by the ethical boards of Helsinki University Hospital Women’s Hospital, and the Helsinki Biobank.

The study has been approved by the ethical boards of Helsinki University Hospital Women’s Hospital, and the Helsinki Biobank. The patient samples and clinical data will be pseudonymized to keep anonymity. All potential sensitive data will be stored within the safeguarded data storage services provided by Helsinki University and Helsinki University Hospital. The principles of the General Data Protection Regulation will be followed. All personnel involved in this prospective study have signed the HUH confidentiality disclosure statement.

MOTIVATION LETTER
Applicant´s name: Ziqi Kang
Title of the doctoral thesis (preliminary): Computational investigations of the single-cell spatial tumor microenvironment in ovarian cancer
Briefly describe your studies, work, and other experiences so far. Additionally, provide examples that demonstrate how your history reflects your ability to successfully complete your dissertation within three years.
Beginning with the broad topic of biology from classic botany and zoology, I gradually narrowed my focus to “tissue microenvironment spatial profiling”. My bachelor’s degree is in Biological Science, during which I realized coding skills are necessary for conducting research involving large-scale datasets. Thus, I applied to the master’s program in bioinformatics at Uppsala University. 
I was trained to analyze omics datasets when doing my master’s study and had a summer internship at Uppsala University & SciLifeLab focusing on scRNA-seq and ATAC-seq, which was published on https://doi.org/10.3390/cells12060891. Although I enjoyed the internship, I found cell interaction and localization are important features and I would like to work with datasets that contain spatial information. Then I started my thesis internship at Karolinska Institution and SciLifeLab, where spatial imaging and sequencing techniques are actively developed. I analyzed a Hyperplexed Immunofluorescence imaging mouse glioblastoma dataset and participated in developing an automatized tumor histology phenotypes identification tool. The research experience inspired me, so I stayed as a research assistant at Karolinska Institution for several months and finished the projects.

oma dataset and participated in developing an automatized tumor histology phenotypes identification tool. The research experience inspired me, so I stayed as a research assistant at Karolinska Institution for several months and finished the projects. In the end, the work I participated in produced two papers published in Nature Communications (https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-023-42878-z, https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-024-47185-9). 
I’m confident that my productivity allows me to fulfill the requirement of three publications and complete my dissertation within three years. With the experience of time management, team collaboration, facing challenges, and work-life balance. I built myself a great working strategy that can ensure my success in this 3-year pilot program. 
Looking ahead, where do you envision yourself in 5 to 10 years? How would writing a dissertation at the University of Helsinki, within this project and topic, support your professional and academic goals?
Table 1. Timetable for future 5-10 years 
Currently, my plan is mainly to stay in academia. I enjoyed life and work in Finland, and I’d be very happy to stay for a round of postdocs here. After the first round of postdocs, I’d like to do the second round of postdocs in Switzerland. I was following the research in several Swiss labs (such as Bodenmiller Lab in Zurich and Johanna Joyce Lab in Lausanne), which are quite relevant to the topic in our lab. Although I didn’t mention industry job career in the table, I’m open to both academia and industry after finishing my PhD. 
Writing my dissertation about computational profiling of single-cell spatial tumor microenvironments requires a large number of high-quality datasets and multidisciplinary exchange.
